state the date of Separation, being a date significantly earlier than three years prior to
the death of the Deceased.
77

In the light of the finding that Andrew’s Will in 2005 is not revoked by the Separation
Agreement; that the rectification sought by the Defendant “…so as to terminate all
rights of the Plaintiff under the Will and to correctly state the date of Separation being
a date significantly earlier than three years prior to the death of the Deceased” is
without merit and will not be granted; that the Plaintiff and Andrew were in a
common-law relationship which ended on 1 March 2016, less than three years before
his 9 July 2018 death, and therefore outside the operation of 18(4)(e) of the Wills Act
of Manitoba,9 the counterclaims sought by the Defendant which are predicated on her
entitlement to the administration of the estate of Andrew, have no merit at all, as the
Letters of Administration granted to her on the premise of Andrew’s intestacy, have
been found to have no basis. Thus, to continue her in the position of administratrix
under Letters erroneously issued in any event, would be inconsistent with the Will of
Andrew Clark executed by him in 2005 the formal validity of which has not been
challenged in these proceedings, and the continued validity of which has been upheld
in this judgment.
Disposition:
1. The Plaintiff’s claim therefore succeeds, in the following particulars:
i. A Declaration that the Plaintiff and the Deceased were in a common law
relationship under the law of Manitoba, Canada between August 1998 and
1 March 2016.
ii. A Declaration that the Will was valid and effectual as of the date of its
execution, and the Deceased died testate.
iii. A Declaration that the dispositive provisions of the Will remain in effect
and were not revoked by the Separation Agreement or otherwise.
iv. A Declaration that the Plaintiff is entitled to the beneficial interest in the
Deceased’s Estate.
v. An order that the Letters of Administration be revoked.
9 supra
78

vi. An order requiring the Defendant to account to the Plaintiff for all
dealings and transactions made by her, or on her behalf in the Deceased’s
Estate.
vii. Payment of all proceeds of such dealings and transactions on the taking
of the account.
viii. An injunction restraining the 2nd Defendant from disposing, charging,
otherwise dealing with any of the assets of the Deceased’s Estate whether
inside or outside the jurisdiction of this court.
2. The Defendant’s counterclaim is dismissed in its entirety.
3. The execution of this judgment as it relates to the operation of the company CG
Bytes, is stayed to abide further directions from the Manitoba Court.
4. Costs to be agreed, if not, to be taxed.
SGD: M.M. Agyemang
Chief Justice
79
court: Supreme Court of Turks and Caicos Islands
summary: Court found a common-law relationship ending 1 March 2016; separation agreement did not revoke the deceased’s 2005 will.

title: RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited v Nicole Hart Lathan (CL 17 of 2023) [2026] TCASC 19 (27 March 2026)
text:
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF THE ACTION NO. CL 17/23
TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
BETWEEN:
RBC ROYAL BANK (BAHAMAS) LIMITED Plaintiff
-and-
NICOLE HART LATHAN Defendant
Appearances: Ms Nayasha Hatmin of Wessex Fairchild for the Plaintiff
Mr George Missick of Geordins for the Defendants
Before: The Hon. Mr Justice Anthony S. Gruchot
Hearing Date: 25th February 2026
Venue: Court 1, Supreme Court, Leeward Highway, Providenciales
DECISION
1. On 8 February 2023, the Plaintiff issued an originating summons seeking leave of the Court
under section 77 of the Registered Land Act (Cap 9.01) (‘the RLA’) to rely on variations of
section 72 and 75 of the RLA, as contained in clauses 5c. and 8.d of a charge granted by the
Defendant over parcel 60713/218/K10.
2. By way of background, the Plaintiff bank is the chargee of the subject parcel under a charge
dated 8 November 2013, and registered on 25 November 2013 (‘the Charge’). The Charge
was to secure advances made by the Plaintiff to the Defendant, totalling US$143,500.00.
The Defendant is the registered owner of the parcel and the chargor. The Defendant is in
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 1 of 16

default under the Charge, and the plaintiff is proceeding to enforce its security by way of
sale.
3. Notwithstanding that an Acknowledgement of Service was filed on 28 September 2023, no
request for the issue of a Notice of Hearing was made until 9 October 2025. The matter then
came before the Court on several occasions when adjournments were granted to allow the
Defendant time either to clear the balance or seek refinancing.
4. On 25 February 2026, it became apparent that the Defendant had been unsuccessful in her
endeavours, and the application progressed.
5. Section 75 of the RLA gives a statutory power of sale to a chargee in the event that the
chargor defaults in repayment of the monies due under the charge. The statutory power of
sale requires that the property concerned be sold by public auction, for which no application
to the Court is required; however, the power of sale only arises after the requirements of
sections 64(2) and 72 of the RLA have been complied with.1
6. Section 64(2) RLA provides:
(2) A date for the repayment of the money secured by a charge may be specified
in the charge instrument, and where no such date is specified or repayment is not
demanded by the chargee on the date specified the money shall be deemed to be
repayable three months after the service of a demand in writing by the chargee.
7. Section 72 RLA provides inter alia:
Chargee’s remedies
72. (1) If default is made in payment of the principal sum or of any interest or any
other periodical payment or of any part thereof, or in the performance or
observance of any agreement expressed or implied in any charge, and continues
for one month, the chargee may serve on the chargor notice in writing to pay the
money owing or to perform and observe the agreement, as the case may be.
(2) If the chargor does not comply, within three months of the date of service,
1 If a date for repayment is specified in the charge and a demand for payment is made on the date, section 64(2) is
of no effect; however, in practice, this is rarely the case.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 2 of 16

with a notice served on him under subsection (1), the chargee may—
…
(b) sell the charged property:
…
8. Section 77 of the RLA provides:
Variation of powers
77. The provisions of sections 70(2) and (3), 72, 73, 74 and 75 of this Ordinance
may in their application to a charge be varied or added to in the charge:
Provided that any such variation or addition shall not be acted upon, unless the
court, having regard to the proceedings and conduct of the parties and to the
circumstances of the case, so orders.
9. In the subject charge, sections 72 and 75 were varied as follows:
a. Clause 5.c. of the Charge provides:
“That section 72 of the Ordinance shall be varied and extended pursuant
to the provisions in that behalf contained in section 77 of the Ordinance so
that the powers of sale and of appointing a receiver thereby conferred and
the Bank’s right of foreclosure and any other powers and remedies of the
Bank whether hereunder or by Common Law, Ordinance or equity or
otherwise may be exercised by the Bank at any time immediately after a
demand for the repayment has been made to the Chargor or if at the time
of the exercise thereof any case shall have happened in which the Bank is
hereinbefore authorised to take possession of the Charged Property or any
default has occurred in respect of performance of any of the Chargor’s
covenants or obligations herein contained and it shall not be necessary to
give any such notice to the charger as is mentioned in section 72 of the
Ordinance.” (Emphasis added)
b. Clause 8.d. of the Charge provides:
“In accordance with the provisions of section 77 of the Ordinance, upon
the Bank obtaining an Order of the Court permitting this clause to be
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 3 of 16

enforced, Section 75 of the Ordinance shall be varied with respect to this
Charge so as to provide that the Bank in exercising its power of sale may
sell the Charged Property by private treaty instead of public auction if it
shall so determine in its sole and absolute discretion. For the purposes of
such application to the Court for consent to this variation, the Chargor
does hereby acknowledge that it shall be reasonable for the Bank to apply
to the Court to approve the Bank’s right to sell the Charged Property by
private treaty upon the Bank having been unable to sell the charged
property by way of a single public auction.” (Emphasis added)
10. In Meridian Mortgage Corporation Ltd -v- Jon Michael Hayes Shibley2 I referred to
the requirement for serving a notice pursuant to section 64(2) RLA in circumstances where
either the charge does not have a date for repayment or a demand for payment was not made
on the date specified. Most commonly, a section 64(2) notice, demanding payment of the
balance due, must be given before a power of sale arises where there is a default under the
terms of the charge, such as non-payment.
11. In this matter, as in Shibley, no section 64(2) notice has been served, and Ms Hatmin seeks
to rely on a purported service of a section 72 (default) notice, which provided for a period
of three months with which to comply, three months being the statutory demand period
provided by section 64(2). She can only do this if leave is given to rely on the variation of
section 72 such that no section 72 notice is required.3
12. The application is supported by an affidavit of Marcus Samuel, the then Country Manager
of the Plaintiff, sworn on 31 January 2023. In that affidavit, he states:
“The Plaintiff was unable to personally serve the Defendant [with] the Section 72
Notice. By way of alternative, pursuant to the Registered Land Ordinance, the
Section 72 Notice was advertised for three consecutive weeks in The Gazette Turks
and Caicos Islands (sic).
The Section 72 notice was also displayed in a prominent place on the property.”
2 (CL 110 of 2021) [2022] TCASC 37 (18 October 2022)
3 See Shibley; Paradise Manor Limited (in liquidation) W.M. Becker and M.L. Becker -v- Bank of Nova Scotia 1984-85
CILR 437.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 4 of 16

13. Exhibited to the affidavit are three photographs. The first is of an envelope affixed to a
chained and padlocked gate, which has stapled to it a document titled ‘Acknowledgment of
Service receipt, printed out on the letterhead of the Plaintiff’s attorneys. This document
reads:
“MATTER: NOTICE (Section 72(1) Registered Land Ordinance)
Your indebtedness to RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited”
14. It then has the Defendant’s name and US address and continues:
“I ACKNOWLEDGE that I have received a copy of the following documents:
DOCUMENT TYPE;
1. Demand Letter from Wessex Fairchild Attorneys dated 22nd July 2021
2. Demand Notice dated 22nd July 2021,”
15. There is then space provided for the insertion of the date served and received, to type or
print the name of the person served, and space for their signature. There is also space to
insert who served the document and the time and place of service. Those spaces are blank.
16. The second photograph is a wider shot of double gates chained and padlocked and showing
the above envelope affixed to the gate. The third photograph is a wider shot still, again
showing the envelope affixed to the gate and also showing a large skip or container beyond
the gates and a number of condominiums beyond. It is clear from the photographs, and was
confirmed by counsel, that the condominiums are not occupied and are derelict to the extent
that a substantial portion of the roof is missing, as are the windows. I was told that the
development had been significantly damaged by Hurricane Irma, which hit the Turks and
Caicos Islands in September 2017.
17. Section 151 RLA provides:
Service of notices
151. A notice under this Ordinance shall be deemed to have been served on or
given to any person––
(a) if served on him personally; or
(b) if served on an attorney holding a power of attorney whereunder such
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 5 of 16

attorney is authorised to accept such service; or
(c) if sent by registered post to him at his last known postal address in the
Islands or elsewhere and a receipt purporting to have been signed by him has
been received in return; or
(d) if service cannot be effected in one of the above-mentioned ways, by
displaying it in a prominent place on the land affected and by publishing it in
three consecutive issues of the Gazette.
18. The Defendant opposes the application on the basis that no power of sale has arisen due to
the fact that the Plaintiff has failed to show that the section 72 notice was lawfully served.
The following issues are taken:
a. No explanation is given as to why the Defendant could not be personally served.
b. The affidavit of Marcus Samuel does not say who served the notice.
c. There is no affidavit from whoever served the notice.
d. There is no indication when and where the notice was served.
e. The whereabouts of the gate to which the envelope is affixed is not identified, and
as such, the premises are not identified.
f. What was affixed to the gate is not a section 72 notice.
19. As such, Mr Missick submits that there is no evidence of proper service of the notice and in
consequence the Plaintiff has not discharged its statutory obligation and therefore no power
of sale has arisen.
20. Ms Hatmin, in reply, submits:
a. That the document described in paragraphs 13 to 15 above says what is in the
envelope.
b. That the failure of Mr Samuel is him not saying how he knew that the notice had
been served, not that it had not been served.
c. That by affixing the notice to the gate it was intended that it would come to the
attention of the Defendant.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 6 of 16

d. That the Defendant has not said that the notice did not come to her attention.
e. That what is being challenged is the strength of the evidence and that there is
sufficient evidence that the Court can find, on the balance of probabilities, that the
notice did come to the attention of the Defendant.
f. That when the proceedings were served, the Defendant entered an appearance by
returning an acknowledgement of service form.
21. Ms Hatmin was unable to say how the proceedings were served. This action was
commenced before the implementation of electronic filing, and this is a file that was
subsequently scanned onto the system; the Court’s hard copy file was not available to me.
There was nothing on the Court electronic file that suggested how the proceedings were
served.
22. Shortly after the hearing, on the same day, Ms Hatmin advised the Court that an application
for service out of the jurisdiction had been made and was granted by way of an order made
on 24 April 2023, and uploaded the relevant documents to the Court's electronic filing
system. That application was supported by an affidavit of Elise James (legal assistant with
the Plaintiff’s attorneys), sworn on 27 March 2023. In that affidavit, Ms James states:
“4. The Plaintiff understands that the Defendant no longer reside[s] in the
jurisdiction. The Defendant resides at … Raleigh, North Carolina, United States
of America….
5. In respect of service against the Defendant, it may be that personal service will
be possible at the residential address of the Defendant referred to in paragraph 4
above.”
23. Ms James does not explain the source of the Plaintiff’s knowledge, nor when this knowledge
was acquired, which given Mr Samuel’s evidence set out in paragraph 12 above, logically
must be after the date when the purported service of the section 72 notice was effected;
however, I note that the address for the Defendant given by Ms James is the same address
for the Defendant as appears in the Charge and on the purported section 72 notice.
24. No application for substituted service was made, and no affidavit of service explaining how
service was effected has been filed. No issue has been taken by the Defendant regarding
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 7 of 16

proper service, so I must assume that the proceedings were personally served on the
Defendant in the USA, as required by Ord. 10 r.5, Civil Rules 2000.
Discussion
25. As noted in paragraph 17 above, the RLA stipulates how notices under the RLA are to be
served or deemed to be served. The provision relied upon by the Plaintiff (section 151(d)
RLA) is, in my view, a provision that gives relief, or a form of substituted service, when
service cannot be effected in accordance with any of the foregoing subsections. It is,
therefore, incumbent on the Plaintiff to show why none of those foregoing methods of
service could be used.
26. No explanation is given as to why the Defendant was not personally served with the section
72 notice, save for the passing comment from Mr Samuel.4 I agree with Ramsay-Hale CJ in
Nancy Peters -v- Woodville Court Limited5 where in dealing with a challenge to service
on a company, she held:
“14. Before leaving the issue of service of the proceedings, I would just note that
the Defendant's application was brought under a Rule6 which specifically provides
that a failure to serve, in accordance with the statutory provisions for service, does
not render the proceedings a nullity which the defendant is entitled to set aside as
of right.
15. The purpose of the Rule as explained by the learned authors of the White Book,
"was to abolish the distinction between non-compliance with
procedural rules which renders proceedings a nullity and non-
compliance which merely renders proceedings irregular" with the
consequence that "a failure to comply with s 725 (1) of the
Companies Act 1985, which provides that a writ may be served on
a company by leaving it at or sending it by post to the company's
registered office ...does not, even on the basis that s 725(1) were
mandatory (a point being left open), constitute such an irregularity
4 See para. 12 above.
5 (CL 123/2017) [2018] TCASC 6 (20 April 2018)
6 Service of proceedings on a company under the Companies Ordinance.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 8 of 16

in the proceedings as to render them a nullity and entitle the
defendant to have them set aside ex debito justitiae as non-
compliance with section 725 (1) is a mere irregularity within 02 r1
(1)": note 2/1/3.
16. I concur with the view expressed in the same note that,
"02, r.1 should be applied liberally in order, so far as is reasonable
and proper to prevent injustice being caused to one party by
mindless adherence to technicalities [in] the rules of procedure."
17. In the last paragraph the following appears:
"Defective service of proceedings, however gross the defect, and
even a total failure to serve where the existence of the proceedings
is nevertheless known to the defendant is an irregularity which can
be cured by the Court by the exercise of discretion under 0 2 r 1."
18. Those observations are relevant to service of proceedings only.
19. With respect to the service of the Section 72 Demand Notice, it is common
ground that a chargee may only commence proceedings to sell property where a
demand notice has been issued pursuant to section 72 of the Registered Land
Ordinance ("RLO"), served in accordance with Section 151 of the RLO and the
money owing remains outstanding at the end of the time limited for payment. (My
emphasis)
27. Service of the section 72 RLA notice had to be effected in accordance with section 151
RLA. The provisions of section 151(d) cannot be utilised unless service under subsections
151(a) to (c) is not possible, and I do not find that the brief evidence from Mr Samuel is
sufficient to persuade me that service could not be effected under those foregoing
provisions, a fortiori, where it seems the originating process was personally served on the
Defendant and where the Defendant’s address has not changed since the Charge was
granted. I find that no notice under section 72 RLA was properly served, and as such, no
power of sale has arisen. Accordingly, this action is dismissed as it could not be commenced
until the power of sale had arisen.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 9 of 16

28. Before I leave the matter of service, in the event I am wrong on the above finding, I should
deal with the issues raised by Mr Missick in sub-paragraphs 18.b to 18.f above. I see much
force in his argument that even if service under section 151(d), in the circumstances of the
case, was permissible, I could not be sure on the balance of probabilities that the notice had
been duly served. I do not think that it was, for the following reasons:
a. The purported notice is affixed to a gate to a land parcel in which there are a number
of properties, this being a strata condominium development. The Defendant’s
property has the title number 60713/218/K10. This, in itself, indicates that there are
at least 10 property titles behind the gate.7
b. Even if I were to accept that the notice could be affixed to a gate that leads to a
number of properties, I could not be sure that this is the gate which leads to the
Defendant’s property, absent evidence of who affixed it and identified the property.
c. What has been affixed to the gate is not a section 72 RLA notice. It is an envelope
and an undated cover sheet. Whilst the coversheet lists what is purportedly in the
envelope, I have no evidence from anyone who prepared the envelope as to what
was in it.
d. Section 151(d) requires a copy of the notice to be displayed ‘in a prominent place
on the land affected’. I am not of the view that placing a notice in an opaque
envelope is ‘displaying it’. It cannot be seen at all.
29. Finally, I also make an observation which was not raised as an issue by counsel, that there
appears to be a condition precedent in this charge, that the ability to come to the Court to
seek leave to rely on the variation of section 75 to sell by private treaty, only arises after the
Plaintiff has been unable to sell the charged property by way of a single public auction.8
There has been no public auction. Mr Samuel states:
“The charged property has not been advertised for sale by public auction as it has
been judicially recogni[s]ed by the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court that
7 It occurs to me that, at best (and assuming this is the correct strata corporation land), the gate is on the common
property of the strata corporation and not the land charged. It is also possible that this is a separate parcel
altogether, and that the owners of the strata lots have a right of way over the land, but I am hypothesising.
8 See para. 9 above.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 10 of 16

distressed properties rarely sell by public auction in this jurisdiction. Moreover,
the Plaintiff’s attorneys have also advised that its past attempts to sell distressed
properties by public auction have been largely unsuccessful.”
30. In Bank of Butterfield (Cayman) Limited -v- Jervis and Jackson,9 in considering an
application by a chargee for leave to sell by private treaty following default in payment of
a secured loan,10 the court refused to grant leave as no public auction had been held before
the application was made. Smellie CJ held at paragraphs 5 to 7:
“As no order of the court is required for the chargee to exercise the power of sale
by public auction in keeping with s. 75 the court’s sanction is indeed required for
a sale by private treaty. It is the long-settled practice of this court that the variation
of the Law by way of the grant of leave to sell by private treaty instead of by public
auction will not be given until after a fair attempt by the chargee to sell by public
auction. The reason for this is obvious and important, and I will explain it as
follows.
Section 75 imposes a duty upon the chargee to act in good faith when exercising
the right to sell by public auction. One aspect of that duty requires the chargee to
seek to get the best price the market will yield (“the market price”). In order to do
so the chargee will obtain an independent valuation and seek to market the
property for sale by public auction at that valuation price or some other reserve
price that the chargee might set pursuant to section 75, but being mindful at all
times the such a reserve price can only be justified as being that which the market
will actually yield at the time.
Until that attempt to sell by public auction has been taken, the court can have no
way of knowing whether some other lower price to be fixed as the reserve price
for Sale by private treaty is a reasonable price.” (Emphasis added)
9 [2011 (1) CILR 54]. The reasons given for the decision were to be applied mutatis mutandis in Cayman National
Building Society -v- Wellington (Cause No.459 of 2010) in which Smellie CJ refused a similar application on the
same day.
10 Sections 64, 72, 75 and 77 of the Cayman Islands Registered Land Law are in identical terms as those in the RLA,
save that the RLA had the words ‘of this Ordinance’ added in section 77 when referring to the section numbers –
see para. 8 above.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 11 of 16

31. However, in Cayman National Building Society -v- Cranston11 Quin J noted12
submissions made by counsel for the plaintiff, as follows:
“Directing a chargee to instruct and pay attorneys to fix an auction, to draft terms
and conditions of sale, and to advertise and hold an auction will involve
considerable professional fees and significant time. This could well act to the
detriment of the chargee bank but more importantly will inevitably act the
detriment of the chargor, who will be settled with the professional costs for the
public auction, and further disadvantaged by delay and interest which will keep
accruing.”
32. In Cranston, counsel for the plaintiff suggested that the mischief which concerned the
learned Chief Justice in Jervis, was that the court was being asked to fix a reserve price
which might be less than the ‘market value’, as at paragraph 7, he continued:
“Yet the imprimatur of the court is what is ultimately sought by the chargee to
absolve it of its duty to obtain the best price when the court sanctions the sale at a
price less than that which the market with persistent effort over time might yield.”
(My emphasis)
33. It seems that in the Cayman Islands, a practice has developed that, before exercising a power
of sale, a chargee would first come to the court for a declaration that the chargor was in
default and seek the sanction of the court as to the price at which the property was to be
offered for sale. The headnote in Scotiabank & Trust (Cayman) Limited -v- Ebanks and
Gordon13 reads:
“A practice had developed whereby chargees seeking to exercise their power of
sale would apply to the court by originating summons for a declaration that the
chargeor was in default and present the evidence of a licensed appraiser or
chartered surveyor as to (i) the true current market value of the property; and (ii)
the market value if the property had to be sold within a short period of time. The
court could then sanction the listing of the property with a licensed real estate
11 [2011 (1) CILR 67].
12 At para. 26.
13 [2012 (1) CILR 401]
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 12 of 16

agent on the Multiple Listing Service at a list price at least as high as (and often
higher than) the true market value and, if time passed without a sale, could later
authori[s]e the chargee to list the property at a lower price.”
34. It is not common practice in the Turks and Caicos Islands to seek sanction from, or for the
Court to fix, a reserve price for which a property is to be offered at auction or for the price
at which it is to be offered for sale by private treaty. As was argued in Cranston, a chargee
remains under an obligation to obtain the best possible price for a property, in compliance
with its section 72 statutory obligation to ‘act in good faith and have regard to the interests
of the chargor’, and if it does not, then the chargor will have a remedy against it.
35. In Barclays Bank PLC -v- Henry and Another14 whilst the application was for leave to
sell by private treaty, to which there was no dispute and both parties agreed that was the
preferred way forward, the issue was the sale price. Ground CJ considered:
a. whether the Court has power on such an application to approve a specific
transaction, or a specific price; and
b. if it does, whether it is appropriate on the facts of this case to approve the proposed
sale price.
36. Ground CJ did not make any specific finding that the Court did or did not have the power,
but declined to sanction the proposed transaction and referred to the duties imposed on a
chargee when exercising its power of sale. He made the following observation with respect
to section 77:
“I think it is important to note that this provision does not confer upon the Court
the power to alter the statute, nor does it confer in terms the power to approve the
sale, or a sale at a particular price. What it empowers the Court to do is to give
its sanction to the agreement between the parties overriding the statutory
provisions. In other words, if the contract between the parties has been embodied
in the charge, and that permits sale by some means other than public auction, then
that can prevail over the statutory restriction but only if the court decrees that it
may be acted upon.
14 (CL 80 of 1999) [1999] TCASC 3 (15 December 1999).
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 13 of 16

37. In Cranston, Quin J observed that section 77 gives the court a wide discretion and that it
does not state that the variation in the charge can only be acted upon after there has been an
unsuccessful auction. He agreed with counsel’s submission that the judgment in Jervis
“could unnecessarily restrict the court’s discretion and is not what the legislation intended”.
38. It appears that Smellie CJ was not referred to Bank of Nova Scotia Trust Co. (Cayman)
Ltd. -v- Bodden.15 Quin J says that he was provided with a full written judgment of Moody
J in that matter, by the consulting editor of The Cayman Islands Law Reports and makes
reference that the same argument was before the court, namely, that the application for leave
to sell by private treaty could only be brought after exhausting the power of sale by public
auction. He references16 the ratio of the judgment extracted by the learned editors of The
Cayman Islands Law Reports,17 which reads:
“As the power of sale given by the mortgage itself was effectively one to sell either
by public auction or private contract, it was not necessary to offer the property for
sale by public auction first and the court would order that the mortgagee be at
liberty to sell by private contract immediately.”
39. In my view the Court has the power to grant leave to sell by private treaty, absent an
unsuccessful public auction first being held, which the Court has done on numerous
occasions, albeit the reasoning not having been expounded as far as I am aware, and I adopt
the views of Ground CJ, as mooted by Mr Samuel;18 however, I do not think it would be
right to grant leave to sell by private treaty in circumstances where the Charge specifically
requires a public auction to have taken place first, as in this case.
40. I have set out in paragraph 9 above the provision of the Charge which varies section 75 of
the RLA. It expressly provides:
“For the purposes of such application to the Court for consent to this variation,
15 19 August 1997 - Unreported.
16 At para. 35.
17 (1952-79 CILR N-8).
18 Ground CJ in Barclays Bank PLC -v- Henry and Another (CL 80 of 1999) [1999] TCASC 3, where he says: “It is
perhaps unfortunate, in this day and age and in the context of a small island jurisdiction, that the Ordinance still
insists upon sale by public auction… I can well understand, and accept that a sale by public auction is often not
feasible and may also be in the interest of neither party because it tends to produce a sale at an undervalue, if a
sale can be achieved at all.”
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 14 of 16

the Chargor does hereby acknowledge that it shall be reasonable for the Bank to
apply to the Court to approve the Bank’s right to sell the Charged Property by
private treaty upon the Bank having been unable to sell the charged property by
way of a single public auction.” (Emphasis added)
41. The Plaintiff must be taken to have been aware of this provision, although it has not been
addressed by Mr Samuel or counsel. I would make the observation that it seems that the
applicable principle would be that laid down by the Court of Appeal in Paradise Manor
Limited (in liquidation) W.M. Becker and M.L. Becker -v- Bank of Nova Scotia19 that,
as per Zacca P.,20 where variations had been agreed “in their contractual agreements, it
cannot be said that it would be unfair or unreasonable, having regard to all the
circumstances, for the learned trial judge to have considered a variation of s.72 under the
summons before him.” And as per Kerr JA,21 “[t]he Agreements were between the parties
at arm’s length with legal advice at their beck and call. The applicant was asking for no
more than what was agreed. It could never be urged with reason and good sense that a
party is taken by surprise, when the other party in whose favour stipulations are made, asks
that effect be given to such stipulations.”22
42. Accordingly, this application has been brought prematurely. The Plaintiff could not come
to the Court until it had held a public auction which did not produce a buyer.
43. In conclusion, I thank counsel for their assistance in this matter and acknowledge that this
was a matter Ms Hatmin inherited and that she was not responsible for the errors and
omissions I have found.
Disposition
44. The Originating Summons dated 7 February 2023 and issued on 8 February 2023 is
dismissed. The Plaintiff shall pay the Defendant’s costs of and occasioned by the application
to be taxed on the standard basis if not agreed and I record the view of the Court that the
costs of this application should not be visited upon the chargor by reliance on the terms of
19 1984-85 CILR 437.
20 At [452] 15.
21 At [465] 15.
22 See also Cranston at paras. 43 and 44.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 15 of 16

the Charge.
27th March 2026
The Hon. Justice Anthony S. Gruchot
Judge of the Supreme Court
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 16 of 16
court: Supreme Court of Turks and Caicos Islands
summary: Whether substituted service under s151(d) suffices and if private-treaty sale may proceed without a prior auction.

title: Alvirto Smith v Shalia Gardiner (CL APM 03/23) [2026] TCASC 17 (11 March 2026)
text:
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF THE ACTION NO. CL APM 03/23
TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
BETWEEN:
ALVIRTO ALI ALEXANDER SMITH APPELLANT
-and-
SHALIA GARDINER RESPONDENT
Before: The Hon. Mr Justice Anthony S. Gruchot
Appearances: Mr Smith pro se
Ms Gardiner pro se
Hearing Date: 14th July 2025
Venue: Supreme Court Annexe, Leeward Highway, Providenciales.
JUDGMENT
1. This is an appeal from the decision of HH Keri-ann Kemble, Resident Magistrate, given
on 30 October 2023, where she ordered Mr Smith to pay Ms Gardiner the sum of
US$7,000.00 by way of refund for monies paid to Mr Smith on 11 October 2022, for the
purchase of a 2008 Nissan Dualis SUV motor vehicle.
2. The learned Magistrate held:
“It stood out to the Court that the contract was never completed.
It is a logical condition of the contract for sale of a vehicle that legal ownership
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 1 of 16

be transferred to the purchaser to afford the purchaser the full benefit of
ownership i.e. the ability to sell and transfer legal ownership of the vehicle to
another. Indeed, the purchaser having paid in full for the vehicle could sue to
have the seller transfer legal ownership. In this case to date, the vehicle is still
in the name of the Defendant…
Instead of arrangements being made to complete the contract by attending [at]
Road Traffic and doing the necessaries – the Defendant proceeded instead to
ignore the Plaintiff over a period of time…
This failure on the Defendant’s part to fully transfer the vehicle to the Plaintiff
is a breach of contract.”
The Evidence
3. In or around September 2022, the Appellant, Mr Smith, advertised a 2008 Nissan Dualis
motor vehicle for sale at an asking price of US$8,800.00. The advertisement stated that
the vehicle was to be sold ‘as is’.
4. The Respondent, Ms Gardiner, knew Mr Smith and contacted him with a view to her
purchasing the vehicle. Ms Gardiner went to see the vehicle and was advised by Mr Smith
that the vehicle could not be started, but that he was going to take it to his mechanic and
have some work done on the vehicle. A price of US$7,500.00 was agreed. Mr Smith
advised that he was going to get the vehicle serviced.
5. On 11th October 2022, the car was ready and Mr Smith took it to Ms Gardiner’s home. Ms
Gardiner took the vehicle for a test drive and was concerned that the engine maintenance
lights were illuminated. She says that Mr Smith said that they would turn themselves off
after she had driven for a while. Mr Smith says that the lights were on because the car had
needed a service. That is why he had taken it to his mechanic, who had not reset the
warning lights. He said that there was a sequence which could be followed to reset the
lights, and he told Ms Gardiner that there was a video on ‘YouTube’ which she could
follow. Ms Gardiner also says that whilst on the test drive, she started hearing a loud sound
and asked Mr Smith what the noise was. She says that Mr Smith said to her that she would
hear that from time to time as a result of the work carried out by his mechanic. These
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 2 of 16

events did not appear to alert Ms Gardiner that there were issues with the vehicle, nor that
she perhaps should have it looked over by a mechanic of her choosing.
6. It is common ground that Ms Gardiner was US$500.00 short when the vehicle was
delivered to her, and Mr Smith agreed to accept the US$7,000.00 as full payment, and he
gave Ms Gardiner a receipt.
7. Ms Gardiner says that Mr Smith said he would draw up a contract and/or bill of sale the
next day. Mr Smith said that was not the case and that they both had to attend the
Department of Motor Vehicles (‘DMV’) to have the vehicle licensed, registered in her
name and to transfer title. Mr Smith agrees that he told Ms Gardiner that the vehicle
insurance and registration were up-to-date, so that she could drive the car without issue
until they could attend at the DMV. He also confirmed that he agreed that he would go to
DMV with Ms Gardiner in order to transfer the registration, but this did not occur.
8. Ms Gardiner complains that she did not receive any paperwork for the vehicle, no
registration, title or insurance documentation.
9. The next day, Ms Gardiner was using the vehicle, and it shut off. This happened on more
than 1 occasion and she got her mechanic to take a look. There is some dispute over the
work that was carried out by Mr Smith’s mechanic and that which Ms Gardiner’s
mechanic thought was required, but that is not something which the Court has to decide.
10. Ms Gardiner says that she tried to contact Mr Smith by messaging him, but that he didn’t
respond. In the end, she decided to take the vehicle to a reputable garage, having described
her mechanic as a ‘bush mechanic’.
11. Ms Gardiner put into evidence an inspection report dated 25 October 2022 from D and B
Auto Repair, which listed a significant number of parts that the vehicle needed replacing,
including front and rear sub-frames, engine mounts, new axles, shock absorbers, etc. No
cost estimate was provided. The inspection report also stated that the rear brake lines were
leaking and that the vehicle was not safe to drive. No challenge has been made to the
report and findings. I therefore accept that it records an accurate state of condition.
12. Ms Gardiner then says she tried to contact Mr Smith on a number of occasions, but she
was unable to get any response. As a result, on 28 October 2022, she took the vehicle to
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 3 of 16

Mr Smith’s property and parked it in his yard, and requested her money back by text
message, in the belief that she thought she had the right to do so.
13. A Mr Kendrick Gardiner Jr gave evidence at the trial before the learned Magistrate. He is
Mr Smith’s cousin and was house-sitting for Mr Smith when the vehicle was returned. He
stated that he noticed the vehicle had been left at Mr Smith’s property on 26 October 2022,
at which time he says he contacted the police, but they did not take any action. He says
around a month later, he called the police again and he was advised to get a tow truck to
have the vehicle removed. Accordingly, he called a tow truck who removed the vehicle
on or about 30 November 2022.
14. Mr Smith in his evidence before me said that he contacted his cousin and told him to
contact the police and then have the vehicle towed. He said the vehicle had been there for
6 weeks, but that he made no contact with Ms Gardiner as she had indicated that she was
going to bring this case. Mr Smith also says that he did not know what happened to the
vehicle after it had been towed and that he did not pay the towing company to remove the
vehicle.
15. Ms Gardiner now says, although it was not mentioned in the learned Magistrate’s notes
of evidence, that she has contacted the towing company and has been told that the vehicle
has been broken up and the parts sold off.
16. The vehicle is now a total loss.
The Appeal
17. I set out my opinion as to how an appeal from the Magistrate is properly put before the
Court in Fritz Jean-Francois -v- Leonorah Missick1. This appeal was commenced
before that judgment was handed down and so has been brought as I set out in paragraph
16 of that judgment. The learned Magistrate delivered her decision on 30 November 2023.
A notice of appeal was filed in the Magistrate’s Court, and Mr Smith entered into a
recognisance to prosecute the appeal on the same day. The notice of appeal was served on
Ms Gardiner on 3 November 2023, within the 5 days allowed.
1 [2024] TCASC 18; CL APM 1/23.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 4 of 16

18. An appeal from the Magistrate to the Supreme Court is by way of rehearing2. The appeal
is heard on the record of evidence considered by the previous decision maker and the Court
has the power to receive further evidence on questions of fact.3
19. I took further evidence from both Ms Gardiner and Mr Smith.
Discussion
20. Whilst Ms Gardiner provided extensive evidence regarding the vehicle's condition, its
issues with breaking down, and alleged representations made by Mr Smith, which the
Court is asked to infer as misrepresentations, this claim is not based on those grounds. The
learned Magistrate also did not rule in favour of Ms Gardiner due to breach of
representation, but on the finding that Mr Smith failed to transfer legal title.
21. Absent any expressed representation as to quality, then the maxim ‘caveat emptor’4
applies. Section 16 of the Sale of Goods Act (Cap. 17.10) (‘SGA’) provides:
Implied terms about quality or fitness
16. (1) Except as provided by this section and section 17,5 and subject to any
other enactment, there is no implied condition or warranty about the quality or
fitness for any particular purpose of goods supplied under a contract of sale.
22. Implied terms as to quality and fitness for purposes are only implied where the goods are
sold in the course of a business.6 There is no suggestion that Mr Smith was acting in the
course of a business, and this was a private sale.
23. Ms Gardiner stated that she did not carry out any checks on the roadworthiness of the
vehicle, and in response to a question from the Court, she conceded that Mr Smith did not
say that the vehicle was in good order, but that he was taking the car to his mechanic to
make sure it was ok. Ms Gardiner confirmed that she did not speak to Mr Smith’s
mechanic. Ms Gardiner then backtracked and said that Mr Smith said he was making sure
that the vehicle was ‘in good working order’. Ms Gardiner also confirmed that Mr Smith
2 Supreme Court Practice 1999 – O.55 – Note 55/0/2
3 Supreme Court Rules 2000 - O.55 r.7.
4 Let the buyer beware. In short, a private buyer buys the goods at his/her own risk.
5 Sale by sample.
6 Sub-sections 16(2) and (3) SGA.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 5 of 16

did not tell her that any of the issues found by the D and B Auto Repair later inspection
were in good order.7
24. Ms Gardiner stated that she did not get any paperwork for the vehicle other than a receipt
for the monies paid. She said that Mr Smith said he would give her a contract and bill of
sale, but these never materialised, and as a result, she asserts that legal title to the vehicle
was never transferred into her name. Ms Gardiner also says that she never received any
title document or registration document for the vehicle. It is on this basis that Ms Gardiner
says the contract was never concluded, with which the learned Magistrate agreed.
25. The four elements for the formation of a contract are trite law. There must be:
a. An offer. In this case an offer to purchase the vehicle at a cost of US$7,000.00 was
made.
b. Acceptance. Mr Smith accepted Ms Gardiner’s offer8 to buy the vehicle at a price
of US$7,000.00.
c. Consideration. Mr Smith accepted the payment of US$7,000.00, gave a receipt and
delivered up the vehicle to Ms Gardiner. There was, I find, good consideration by
both parties, i.e. the payment of money and the delivery up of the vehicle.
d. An intention to create a legal relationship. This is not in dispute. Mr Smith intended
to sell the vehicle to Ms Gardiner, and Ms Gardiner intended to buy it.
26. The learned Magistrate held:
“It was not in issue that there was a contract between the parties concerning the
Nissan.”
27. In ordinary circumstances, under the common law, absent any additional terms of
agreement, the above steps would have concluded the contract, i.e. title would have passed
to Ms Gardiner upon the irrevocable agreement to buy and sell the vehicle, or at the latest,
upon delivery to Ms Gardiner.
7 See para. 11 above.
8 The vehicle had been advertised for sale at US$8,000.00. The Respondent offered US7,500.00, but when it came
to payment, only had US$7,000.00. The Appellant agreed to accept the US$7,000.00 in full payment.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 6 of 16

28. The issues for the Court, as asserted by Ms Gardiner, are whether promises were made by
Mr Smith to give to Ms Gardiner:
a. a written contract and a bill of sale;
b. the registration document for the vehicle;
c. a title document; and
d. insurance documents.
were terms of the contract for the sale of the vehicle, and if so, did not producing these
documents mean that the legal title to the vehicle had not been vested in Ms Gardiner, as
the learned Magistrate found.
29. Mr Smith, in his evidence, stated that he was of the understanding that they had to go to
DMV in order to complete a ‘Bill of Sale’ to transfer title and register the vehicle in Ms
Gardiner’s name.
The Road Traffic Act (Cap. 13.01)
30. The Road Traffic Act (‘RTA’) and the regulations made thereunder have certain
provisions dealing with the registration and licensing of motor vehicles.
31. Section 5 RTA provides inter alia:
Licensing and registration of motor vehicles
(1) Every motor vehicle or trailer imported into the Islands before being used
otherwise on the road shall be taken from the point of importation direct to an
inspector appointed under section 3(2)(a) (hereinafter referred to as a “vehicle
inspector”), or direct to a warehouse, for examination, and no such vehicle shall
be used further on the road otherwise than by a vehicle inspector for testing
purposes until a vehicle inspector has certified the vehicle fit for use on the road
and has issued in respect of the vehicle a prescribed certificate of
roadworthiness.
(2) Any person who uses or permits to be used on a road any motor vehicle or
trailer for which any licence under this Part has not been issued or has expired
commits an offence.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 7 of 16

(5) The Director, on being satisfied that the provisions of the Motor Vehicles
(Third Party Insurance) Ordinance have been complied with shall, on receipt of
payment of the prescribed fee, register the motor vehicle by assigning it a serial
registration number, recording the prescribed particulars in the register, and shall
issue to the owner an identification plate and a licence in the prescribed form.
… (Emphasis added)
32. Section 9 RTA provides:
(5) Where a registered vehicle is sold or otherwise transferred the transferor shall
so inform the Director in the prescribed form within seven days after the transfer
and shall remove and retain the identification plate of the vehicle, and the
transferee shall submit to the Director the application for registration and the
vehicle licence. (Amended by Ords. 1 of 2003 and 13 of 2019) (Emphasis added)
(6) The Director shall consider the application for registration of the vehicle as
if it were an application, mutatis mutandis, under section 5, but shall not issue
an identification plate in respect of the vehicle where the transferee has already
been issued an identification plate which is not assigned to a registered vehicle,
in which case the transferee shall affix that identification plate to the vehicle in
the prescribed manner, and the transferor of the vehicle shall, upon becoming
the registered owner of another vehicle, affix the identification plate to that
vehicle in the prescribed manner.
33. Section 62 RTA provides:
Regulations
62. (1) The Governor may make regulations prescribing any matter which by
this Ordinance may be prescribed and concerning any matter which appears to
him to be necessary or desirable for giving effect to this Ordinance.
Road Traffic Regulations (‘the Regulations’)
34. Regulation 46 provides:
Transfer of registration and fee
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 8 of 16

46. (1) Subject to section 9(4) of the Ordinance, on the change of ownership of
a motor vehicle, the registered owner and the new owner shall sign the form set
out in Schedule 8 and the transferee shall submit the form to the Director together
with the fee of $20. (Emphasis added)
35. Regulation 46 is expressly subject to section 9(4) of the Act. Section 9(4) deals with the
offences concerning identification plates. It is section 9(5) which deals with the sale or
other transfer of a registered vehicle. It appears that the reference to section 9(4) of the
RTA is an error.
36. Regulation 47 provides:
Transfer of ownership
47. On the transfer of ownership of a motor vehicle, the Director shall amend the
register, the registration and the licence. (Inserted by L.N. 40/2019)
37. The form set out in Schedule 8 (‘the Prescribed Form’) is titled ‘Transfer of Motor
Vehicle’, albeit I take judicial notice that this is colloquially known as a ‘Bill of Sale’.9 It
provides inter alia for details to be given of the seller, the buyer, the price paid and details
of the vehicle and current registration number. It is to be signed by both the seller and the
buyer, and a witness. As noted in paragraph 34 above, it is for the transferee (or buyer) to
submit the form.
38. There is therefore an apparent tension between the Act and the Regulations. Under the
Act, it is for the seller to inform the Director10 of the sale in the Prescribed Form within 7
days after the transfer. Under the Regulations, the transferee (or the buyer) is to submit
the Prescribed Form.
39. The Act also provides that the purchaser must submit an application for registration and
for a vehicle licence. In doing so, section 5 RTA is triggered, as if the vehicle has just
been imported. When the registration/licensing process is completed, the vehicle is
registered and licensed to the purchaser.
9 As referred to by Mr Smith – see para. 29 above.
10 The Director of Road Safety and Transportation – See section 2 of the RTA.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 9 of 16

40. I was not addressed as to whether the submission of a completed Prescribed Form by the
buyer is accepted by the Director as informing him of the sale or other transfer of a vehicle.
If it is not accepted, then as Mr Smith appears to believe, it would seem that both the seller
and the buyer need to attend at the DMV. Whether they need to attend at the same time is
unclear.
41. The above process does not require the seller to deliver to the purchaser the seller’s
registration form for the vehicle. Ms Gardiner would get a new registration in her name.
Likewise, a seller is not in my view, under any obligation to deliver up their insurance
documents.
42. The learned Magistrate based her decision on the fact that Mr Smith never attended at the
DMV and as such, did not complete the contract. She must therefore have considered that
Mr Smith:
a. was obligated to attend as a contractual obligation; and
b. that he declined or otherwise neglected to do so;
However, the learned Magistrate recorded:
“The defendant testified in examination in chief:
‘Plans were made Ms Gardiner and I to attend the Road Traffic Department to
have the transfer documents and contract completed. However, this never
happened. We were to go to Road Safety anytime Ms Gardiner was ready to go
to Road Safety but she and I would have to go there. No date had been agreed
at that time. I never spoke to Ms Gardiner after the day I give her the receipt
about having the contract completed.’”
43. Ms Gardiner, in her evidence before me, said that there was no firm arrangement to attend
at the DMV and that when she found there were issues with the vehicle, she returned it to
Mr Smith, by parking it in his yard and then messaged him, asking for her money back.
44. There are, therefore, 2 different issues. The learned Magistrate found that there had been
a breach of contract by non-attendance at the DMV, but Ms Gardiner was of the view that
she could reject the vehicle due to the faults and demand her money back, and as such
never asked Mr Gardiner to attend at the DMV.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 10 of 16

45. I am of the view that if there is a requirement for both parties to attend at the DMV and
Mr Smith refused or neglected to do so, then the learned Magistrate was correct to find a
breach of contract, although not one which affected the legal title, to which I return below.
I come to that conclusion on the basis that if the local conditions require attendance, then
such attendance would be an implied term of the contract; however, I cannot, on the
evidence, find on the balance of probabilities that Mr Smith neglected or refused as events
took over and Ms Gardiner sought to reject the vehicle.
46. I am not of the view that the process required by the RTA and by the DMV has any effect
on legal title to the vehicle. Legal title to a motor vehicle is, in my view, no different to
legal title to any other property and title transfers when the parties intend it to transfer.
Section 19 SGA provides:
Property passes when intended to pass
19. (1) Where there is a contract for the sale of specific or ascertained goods the
property in them is transferred to the buyer at such time as the parties to the
contract intend it to be transferred.
(2) For the purposes of ascertaining the intention11 of the parties, regard shall be
had to the terms of the contract, the conduct of the parties and the circumstances
of the case.
47. The purpose and process required by the RTA of registration and licensing of a vehicle
after a sale within the Islands is to record the transfer of title of a vehicle (or ownership)
and to allow the use of the vehicle on public roads. The form set out in Schedule 8 of the
Road Traffic Regulations is to inform the Director that a transfer of ownership has taken
place so that he may discharge his duty under Regulation 47, which provides:
Transfer of ownership
47. On the transfer of ownership of a motor vehicle, the Director shall amend the
register, the registration and the licence.
48. In my view, subsequent registration and licensing after a vehicle has been sold is not
11 Rules for ascertaining intention are set out in section 20 SGA.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 11 of 16

required to transfer the legal title; the emphasised extracts from the RTA and the
Regulations anticipate that legal title has already passed. That said, I can see
administrative issues following a subsequent transfer and attempt to register and licence,
if a former transfer has not been registered.
49. That does not dispose of the matter. I have set out in paragraphs 20 to 22 above that there
are no implied terms in a private contract for sale, as to quality and the fitness for purpose.
In the absence of any statements by the seller that would amount to conditions or
warranties, it is for the buyer to satisfy themselves as to those matters. Ms Gardiner, in
her evidence before me, accepted that Mr Smith had made no comment on any of the
issues contained in the D and B Auto Repair report before she purchased the vehicle. Ms
Gardiner was not, in my view, in a position where she could reject the vehicle and
repudiate the contract or claim damages; however, in attempting to do so, she left the
vehicle at Mr Smith’s premises, and he subsequently disposed of it. However, the matter
does not rest there either.
50. Ms Gardiner’s evidence was that following her leaving the vehicle in Mr Smith’s yard,
she heard nothing further from him despite her attempts to contact him. Mr Smith’s
evidence before me was that he did not contact Ms Gardiner after she had left the vehicle
and before he instructed his cousin to have the vehicle removed.
51. I am of the view that when the vehicle was left at Mr Smith’s property, he became an
involuntary or gratuitous bailee of it; alternatively, by disposing of it, Mr Smith has
converted the vehicle to his own use.
52. An involuntary bailment arises where a person is in possession of goods and aware of the
possession, but has given no consent to it.12
53. I refer to the passages from Halsbury’s Laws of England (2020), Volume 4, Bailment and
Pledge, where the learned authors state at paragraph 112:
“112. Deposit without agreement.
While the orthodox or literal transaction by way of depositum is a consensual
12 See the Encyclopaedia of Form and Precedents – LexisNexis at BAILMENT vol 3(1) > Commentary > F:
INVOLUNTARY BAILMENT AND BAILMENT BY CONCEALMENT 1: at para. 45.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 12 of 16

bailment (deriving from express agreement between bailor and bailee) there
exist certain analogous forms of relationship based on one person's unrewarded
possession of another's goods which, while not directly the product of
agreement, may produce obligations akin to those of an orthodox bailment by
way of depositum and might in some cases have traditionally been viewed as
deriving from an implied agreement as to possession.”
54. And at paragraph 120:
“120. Involuntary deposit.
Where a chattel is sent, without request or arrangement, by one person to
another who does not hold himself out as willing to receive it, the person to
whom it is sent is deemed to be an involuntary bailee and will ordinarily owe no
responsibility to the sender to exercise reasonable care for its safe custody or
protection, but must not deliberately convert it to his own use and must take
reasonable care when purporting to redeliver the chattel to the owner or his
authorised representative (which would otherwise render him liable in
conversion). It has, however, been suggested that an involuntary bailee may owe
a duty of care, the standard varying according to what is reasonable in the
particular circumstances. In most cases, the standard would be low because of
the involuntary nature of the transaction, but in some a higher standard would
apply.”
55. What duty then did Mr Smith owe to Ms Gardiner? The duties of an involuntary bailee
are less than those of a voluntary bailee. The learned authors of the Encyclopaedia of
Forms and Precedents say:13
“The traditional view is that the involuntary bailee owes no duty to take
reasonable care to guard the goods against loss, theft or physical injury.
Provided the involuntary bailee does not attempt to deliver them to a third party,
they need only abstain from wilful or reckless conduct towards them.
…
13 Ibid at para. 46
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 13 of 16

It had been argued that the involuntary bailee’s duty should be one of reasonable
care in all the circumstances, but authority favouring this had been slight.
However, more recent authority is in support of this view— that its obligation in
law as involuntary bailee is to do what is right and reasonable in the
circumstances of the case.”
56. In Scotland -v- Solomon14 David Kitchin QC, sitting as a Deputy Judge of the High
Court15 stated:
“The obligations of an involuntary bailee were explained by Hawke J. in Elvin
and Powell Ltd. v Plummer Roddis Ltd. (1933) 50 TLR 158. If persons are
involuntary bailees and have done everything reasonable they are not liable to
pay damages if something which they do results in the loss of the property. There
is an obligation on the part of involuntary bailees to do what is right and
reasonable. (Emphasis added)
57. The application was an appeal from the Master who had refused summary judgment in
favour of the Solomons, or alternatively, to strike out the claim. The matter concerned the
sale of a property under a charging order. After the property was sold, the Solomons
obtained a possession order, which was executed by the bailiff. Belongings of the
Scotlands’ remained in the property and when the house was sold, the belongings were
either converted to the purchaser or they were disposed of by the purchaser. The Scotlands
brought a claim for the value of the items lost.
58. The court held that if the Scotlands’ case was proved, when the Solomons took possession
of the house, they became involuntary or gratuitous bailees of the possessions, and they
would have been under an obligation to do what was right and reasonable. The court noted
that the Solomons’ solicitor “knew Mr Scotland's new address from early July, so there
was no difficulty in communicating with him to tell him the house was being sold and that
he must remove his possessions if he wanted to”.
14 [2002] EWHC 1886.
15 The principle of law being cited with approval in Da Rocha‑Afodu and another -v- Mortgage Express Ltd and
another [2014] EWCA Civ 454 and Credit Agricole Corporate and Investment Bank -v- Persons with an Immediate
Right to Possess Goods and others [2025] EWHC 1346 (Ch).
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 14 of 16

59. The principle that an involuntary bailee has an obligation to do what is right and
reasonable was further expounded in Da Rocha‑Afodu and another -v- Mortgage
Express Ltd and another16 by Arden LJ, who held that, in considering whether the
involuntary bailee had done what was right and reasonable, the Court had to be alert to all
the particular circumstances in the case. That reasoning was later applied in Campbell -
v- Redstone Mortgages Limited.17
60. In my judgment, Mr Smith was obliged to act reasonably and to do what was right in
respect of how he dealt with the vehicle. He was aware that there was a dispute over the
sale and condition of the vehicle. Ms Gardiner was in contact with him, but he declined
to respond to her. He did not ask Ms Gardiner to remove the vehicle from his yard and did
not give her any notice that he intended to dispose of it.
61. Mr Smith did not act reasonably when he gave instructions to his cousin to have the
vehicle removed without first informing Ms Gardiner of his intentions. As a result, the
vehicle is now a total loss.
62. The question that arises is what, if anything, is Ms Gardiner entitled to recover? The
learned Magistrate found that there was a failure of consideration, in that Mr Smith failed
to transfer title in the vehicle to Ms Gardiner and awarded her the full $7,000.00.
63. For the reasons I have given, I do not agree that there was no transfer of title to Ms
Gardiner, so the Magistrate’s decision cannot stand. Even if the parties had attended at the
DMV and the vehicle had been registered to Ms Gardiner, the real dispute would have
still remained as the vehicle required work to be carried out on it.
64. I have also found on the principle of caveat emptor, that Ms Gardiner did not have a right
to reject the vehicle and demand repayment of the monies paid, however she is now in a
position where she is out of pocket and has lost the vehicle as a result of Mr Smith’s breach
of his obligation. She cannot now have the vehicle put into a roadworthy condition, and it
is on that premise that I approach an assessment of damages.
65. In Scotland, the court held:
16 Ibid.
17 [2014] EWHC 3081 (Ch).
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 15 of 16

“The Master had no doubt, and I agree, that the valuations put on many of the
items by Mr Scotland are grossly exaggerated. Nevertheless, I also agree with
the Master that it is impossible at this stage to say that the items left at the
premises had no value. There is a real issue as to what goods remained at the
premises after the visits in June and what their value was.
66. Unfortunately, the report from D and B Auto Repair, whilst listing the parts that needed
replacing, did not provide any costing for those parts nor any estimate of the costs of
carrying out the work.
67. The price Ms Gardiner paid for the vehicle is a useful starting point for assessing her loss.
In my view, however, the price Ms Gardiner paid does not reflect the value of the vehicle
at the time of the loss. Whilst I often refer to a definition of ‘value’ as ‘being the price a
willing buyer, acting without compulsion, is willing to pay at any given time’, that cannot
apply in this instance. Had Ms Gardiner had the foresight to have the vehicle inspected, it
seems to me that it is plainly evident that she would have negotiated a lower price (or
perhaps not purchased the vehicle at all, but then there would have been no issue with
which to trouble the Court).
68. In my judgment, the appropriate measure of damages is the value of the vehicle in its
unrepaired state, for it is that which has been lost. I was not addressed by either party on
the costs of the parts and work required. I would therefore invite them to make further
submissions on that issue and on the issue of costs.
Disposition
69. The order of the learned Magistrate dated 3 October 2023 is set aside.
70. Judgment is entered for the Plaintiff for damages to be assessed together with costs.
11th March 2026
The Hon. Justice Anthony S. Gruchot
Judge of the Supreme Court
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 16 of 16
court: Supreme Court of Turks and Caicos Islands
summary: Seller converted a sold vehicle by disposing of it, giving the buyer a claim for damages despite title transfer.

title: R v John Smith and Ors (CR 31/24; CR 32/24; CR 33/24) [2026] TCASC 18 (6 March 2026)
text:
IN THE SUPREME COURT
TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
R v JOHN THEODORE SMITH CR 31/24
R v KENNETH MARK ABRAMS CR 32/24
R v ROBERT KOENIG CR 33/24
__________________________________________________________________
DECISIONS ON ABUSE OF PROCESS AND
EXCLUSION OF EVIDENCE APPLICATIONS
__________________________________________________________________
Before His Lordship Justice Chris Selochan.
Appearances: Mr. Andrew Mitchell KC and Ms. Tamika Grant for the Crown
Mr. Alair Shepherd KC and Mr. James Shepherd for John Theodore Smith
Mr. Oliver Smith KC and Ms. Kimone Tennent for Kenneth Mark Abrams
Mr. Jerome Lynch KC and Mr. Mark Fulford for Robert Koenig
Hearing Dates: 24th February 2026, 25th February 2026, 26th February 2026, 27th
February 2026, 2nd March 2026, 3rd March 2026.
Venue: Annex, Supreme Court, Providenciales
Handed Down: 6th March, 2026
1

BACKGROUND
1. This is a part-heard judge alone trial in which the Defendants are on trial for the
following on a five count indictment:
Count 1: Conspiracy to Defraud contrary to Common Law.
Particulars:
John Theodore Smith, Kenneth Mark Abrams and Robert Koenig between 1st October
2013 and 31st March 2022, conspired together with Al Gutman to defraud the Turks
and Caicos Islands Airport Authority by dishonestly procuring and arranging a
contract between TCIAA and LED Industrial Group without adhering to the
applicable procurement procedures whilst failing to disclose their private interest in
LED Industrial Group.
Count 2: Non-Disclosure of Director’s Pecuniary Interest contrary to section 12(2) of
the Airports Authority Act.
Particulars:
John Theodore Smith, between the 1st October 2013 and 1st July 2020, while acting as
an ex-officio Director of the Board of Directors of the Turks and Caicos Airport
Authority, failed to disclose his pecuniary interest in the proposed contract and/or
contract of LED Industrial Group and the Turks and Caicos Islands Airport Authority
and was present at meetings of the Board at which the same was considered and took
part in the consideration of the same.
Count 3: Corruption contrary to section 67(b) of the Integrity Commission Act.
Particulars:
John Theodore Smith, between 1st October 2013 and 1st July 2020, in the performance
of his duty as a public official, namely the Chief Executive Officer of the Turks and
Caicos Airport’s Authority, omitted to perform duties as owed to a public body,
namely the Turks and Caicos Islands Airport Authority [TCIAA], by failing to adhere
to the procurement procedures in his consideration and acceptance of a contract
between LED Industrial Group and TCIAA whilst also failing to disclosure his
personal interest in the said contract, for the purpose of obtaining a benefit for himself
and/or another person Robert Koenig.
2

Count 4: Corruption contrary to section 67(b) of the Integrity Commission Act.
Particulars:
Kenneth Mark Abrams, between 1st October 2013 and 31st March 2022, knowingly or
recklessly allowed his private interest in LED Industrial Group and his personal
relationship with Robert Koenig to improperly influence his conduct in the
performance of his duties as a public official, namely as the Chief Financial Officer of
the Turks and Caicos Islands Airport Authority, by failing to declare is interest in the
proposed contract and/or contract between LED Industrial Group and the Turks and
Caicos Islands Airport Authority.
Count 5: Financial Misconduct contrary to section 62(1) of the Public Procurement Act.
Particulars:
John Theodore Smith, between 1st October 2013 and 1st July 2020, whilst acting as the
Chief Executive Officer of the Turks and Caicos Islands Airport Authority [TCIAA]
committed financial misconduct in respect of the contract with LED Industrial Group.
THE APPLICATIONS
2. The trial of the matter commenced on 15th January 2026 and it has advanced to the
stage where two witnesses have given evidence: Mr. Gordon Burton (former
Chairman of the Board of the TCIAA) and Mr Conroy Smith (Board Member of the
TCIAA). Both are to be recalled for further cross-examination. Prior to the applications
which are the subject of this ruling, the Crown had proposed to call twenty witnesses,
although it was expected that some of the evidence would be agreed, thereby
considerably reducing the length of time that the Crown would take to present its case.
3. Due to the voluminous documentation involved, the matter is being done digitally in
that the Court and defence counsel have each been provided with an iPad on which
Witness Statements, Documents (both used and unused), submissions, the indictment,
and applications are uploaded. Documents which are not to be viewed by the Court
(such as unused materials) are not uploaded to the Judge’s iPad. Users of the iPad
obtain newly uploaded documents by syncing the device when a good internet
connection is available. It follows that newly disclosed materials are uploaded to the
3

Defendants’ iPad and, given the voluminous nature of the documents, it is reasonable
to expect the Crown to immediately formally alert the Defence of the uploading of any
such new disclosure by correspondence such as email or a hand delivered letter.
4. The Applicants have applied for a stay of proceedings pursuant to the Court’s inherent
jurisdiction to stay proceedings on the basis of abuse of process.
5. Without Prejudice to this application, the Second Named Defendant has filed an
application for the exclusion of certain evidence. The First and Third Named
Defendants are also relying on this application.
6. The genesis of the Application to stay the proceedings on the basis of abuse of process
and, in the alternative, to exclude evidence, are applications by the Crown in February
2026 to rely on the following statements by way of additional evidence:
a. Statement of Patrick Woodham.
b. Statement of Stephen Snow.
c. Statements of Peter James labelled as follows:
i. Statement 287A.
ii. Statement 290A.
iii. Statement 292.
d. Statement of Graham Devine.
e. Statement of Malcolm Devereaux.
f. Statement of Barry Greenwood.
g. Statement of Nicquel Garland.
7. On 14th February, 2026, the Crown had filed a Prosecution Note (“the Prosecution
Note”) in which it sought to justify why the additional evidence of Patrick Woodham,
Stephen Snow, Peter James, Graham Devine and Malcolm Devereaux should be
included. Subsequently, the Crown filed applications on 16th February 2026 and 26th
February 2026 in respect of witnesses Barry Greenwood and Nicquel Garland
respectively. I will proceed to canvass the Crown’s arguments in the Prosecution Note.
4

THE PROSECUTION NOTE
8. In the Prosecution Note, the Crown advanced the following arguments for the
additional evidence of Patrick Woodham, Stephen Snow, Peter James, Graham Devine
and Malcolm Devereaux:
a. Statement of Patrick Woodham
Crown’s arguments:
i. This witness will be called to tender the employment contracts of the First Defendant,
John Smith.
ii. These are contracts which Mr. Malcolm Devereaux, a witness the defence agreed could
be read, had not produced.
iii. The contracts are relevant to fill in the contractual picture of the First Defendant’s
employment as CEO.
b. Statement of Stephen Snow
Crown’s arguments:
i. This statement deals with emails spanning the period February 2014 to 2018 between
the Third Defendant, the Second Defendant, Mark Wilkinson and others dealing with
issues relating to the installation of lighting products and promptitude of payments.
ii. A hard drive with these and other materials was served on the Defence team in
June 2025.
iii. This is contemporary evidence of the relationship between the Second Defendant
and the Third Defendant.
c. Statements of Peter James
Crown’s arguments:
Statement 287A
i. Statement 287 was served on the Defence on 13th January 2026.
5

ii. Statement 287A is an update and clarification to statement 287 and needs to be
considered with it.
iii. Statement 287 deals with the invoice material from Fortis/Pelican.
Statement 290A
- This statement corrected some errors in Statement 290 which dealt with an
analysis of LEDIG’s bank account at Citibank and was served on 4th December
2025.
Statement 292
i. This statement was dated 27th November 2025 and was served on the Defence on
11th February 2026. It therefore pre-dates the Order but was served after.
ii. It produces invoices arising from a keyword of email from the Second and Third
Defendants regarding invoices from LEDIG to TCIAA. This material has been
available to the defence for many months.
iii. It formally exhibits PHJ106 which was disclosed to the Defence in the trial papers
on 4th December 2025.
d. Statement of Graham Devine
Crown’s arguments:
i. The Third Defendant’s legal team had prepared an analysis of the data
from the invoices on 23rd January 2026.
ii. The Crown asked Graham Devine about the accuracy and Graham
Devine offered to prepare his own schedule.
iii. This statement explains his work on the spreadsheets in which he
number crunched the LEDIG invoices in relation to the bulbs that he
could compare as like for like to produce spreadsheets.
iv. The material was all available to the defence.
v. All that Graham Devine has done is identify, by using the Third
Defendant’s own numbers, from his invoices and projection sheet, that
the TCIAA paid more than they should have resulting from the
proposed savings to LEDIG.
6

e. Statement of Malcolm Devereaux
Crown’s arguments:
i. This witness’ original evidence was to be read. He produces employment contracts
of the First Defendant and the Second Defendant.
ii. The defence team have raised a serious of questions with the Crown relating
to the contractual relationship that the First Defendant had with TCIAA.
iii. As a result of those questions, the witness was asked about the
employment/personnel file of the First Defendant which he addresses in this
statement.
iv. The evidence is relevant to the questions posed of the Crown by the Defence.
APPLICATIONS TO ADDUCE EVIDENCE OF BARRY GREENWOOD AND NICQUEL
GARLAND
Statement of Barry Greenwood Statement
9. In an Amended Application to Adduce Additional Evidence dated 16th February 2026,
the Crown said the following in respect of Mr. Greenwood’s evidence:
The Crown have now received the statement from Barry Greenwood, an electrician with 35 years’
experience in the energy saving industry. He has considered the work done by Mr Devine, also subject
to the application for admission of additional evidence. His evidence, which the Court would need to
consider includes a critical analysis of (inter alia) the Contract; Terms; project calculations; and LEDIG
invoicing.
His evidence, in conjunction with Graeme Devine’s will assist the court in understanding the whole
process of LEDIG’s approach to this contract and will assist in putting into perspective the working
document, albeit not yet seen by the court, on the energy savings sent by counsel for Mr Koenig.
The case presented by the Crown relies on the relationship between the TCIAA CEO and CFO on the
one hand and RFK (the Third Defendant) on the other, this evidence shows that there could not have
been a proper analysis of the business case and contract which TCIAA entered into, following the
powerful representations of JTS (the First Defendant) to the Board. It also might assist the Court in
7

understanding why persons have had some difficulty, in fact, in understanding the savings
mechanisms.
