As it relates to the likelihood of this evidence causing an inordinate delay, it chiefly relates to
methodology purportedly employed by RFK, and his own expertise in that area. It should not therefore
take a disproportionate amount of time to be considered. Further, we would not propose to call Mr
Greenwood and Mr Devine until the end of our case. [Emphasis mine].
a. The Statement of Nicquel Garland
10. On 26th February 2026, the Crown made a formal application to adduce additional
evidence, namely a statement of Nicquel Garland on the grounds that:
a. Ms. Garland’s evidence formally completes the chain of custody on the Production
Order material as Peter James has already detailed in his statements that he provided.
b. She also exhibits the letter she prepared which was served as PHJ 107(8) in the 4th
December formal service.
THE ABUSE OF PROCESS APPLICATION AND SUPPORTING DEFENCE
SUBMISSIONS
11. In support of its application, reference has been drawn by the Defendants to the dicta
of Dyson JSC in Regina v Maxwell1 in which he stated, inter alia:
“…It is well established that the court has the power to stay proceedings in two categories of
case, namely (i) where it will be impossible to give the accused a fair trial, and (ii) where it
offends the court’s sense of justice and propriety to be asked to try the accused in the particular
circumstances of the case. In the first category of case, if the court concludes that an accused
cannot receive a fair trial, it will stay the proceedings without more. No question of the
balancing of competing interests arises. In the second category of case, the court is concerned
to protect the integrity of the criminal justice system. Here a stay will be granted where the
court concludes that in all the circumstances a trial will offend the court’s sense of justice and
propriety (per Lord Lowry in R v Horseferry Road Magistrates’ Court, Ex p Bennett [1994] 1
1 [2011] 1 WLR 1837 at paragraph 13
8

AC 42, 74 g) or will undermine public confidence in the criminal justice system and bring it
into disrepute (per Lord Steyn in R v Latif [1996] 1 WLR 104, 112 f)…”
12. The application is made pursuant to the first limb in Regina v Maxwell2 in that the
Defendants contend that, notwithstanding the trial judge’s best efforts, the Defendants
have not had a fair trial so far, and submit that the question which the Court has to
consider is whether, viewed cumulatively, disclosure failures, evidential evolution
and what they describe as the ‘mid-trial reformulation of the prosecution case’ have
rendered it impossible to give the Defendants a fair trial, thus requiring the
intervention of the Court.
13. The Defence has submitted a detailed timeline of events leading to the trial, which
comprises of a series of Notes which had been helpfully provided to the Court and all
counsel prior to each hearing by junior counsel for the First Defendant, Mr. James
Shepherd (“the ‘James Shepherd Notes”). Of particular interest are the various
deadlines given by the Court for the completion of disclosure as well as the following
which are worth mentioning:
i. 2nd July 2024: First appearances in court.
ii. May 2025: Mr. Andrew Mitchell KC no longer instructed to prosecute the
matter.
iii. February 2025: First Rule 79 Certificate uploaded.
iv. 15th July 2025: Mr. Martin Hackett confirmed as trial counsel. Trial Date vacated
to 3rd November 2025. Direction given for Rule 79 Certificate to be filed on or
before 18th July 2025.
v. 28th July 2025: Second Rule 79 Certificate uploaded.
vi. August 2025: Mr. Andrew Mitchell KC was re-instructed to prosecute the
matter.
vii. 17th October 2025: iPads distributed and contain same material served in July
2025.
2 Supra at paragraph 8
9

viii. 15th November 2025: The Crown applied for a Production Order to obtain
electricity bills for the airports from electricity provider Pelican Energy3.
ix. 2nd December 2025: The Crown received material from Pelican Energy as a
result of the Production Order.
x. 4th December 2025: Third Rule 79 Certificate was filed.
xi. 15th January 2025: Trial commences and the Crown opens its case.
xii. 18th January 2025: The Defence ‘discover’ material on the Ipad relating to Fortis
billing. No notice had been given to the Defence by the Crown of the uploading
of this material.
xiii. 19th January 2026: The Crown provided a Dropbox link to certain material. The
trial was adjourned until 9th February 2026 for the Defence to consider the
material provided.
xiv. 11th February 2026: The Crown provided a Dropbox link to certain additional
material.
xv. 12th February 2026: Notice of Additional Evidence dated 11th February 2026
was uploaded to the iPads.
xvi. 13th February 2026: Notice of Additional Evidence dated 13th February 2026
was uploaded to the iPads.
xvii. 14th February 2026: The Crown served a Prosecution Note (which it
subsequently referred to as “the Valentine’s Day Note”) and which indicated
that the Crown sought to rely on John Theodore Smith’s employment contracts,
S296 of Stephen Snow, S287(A) of Peter James, S290A of Peter James, S298 of
Graeme Devine and S299 of Devereaux Malcolm4.
xviii. 15th February 2026: Unused materials were uploaded to the iPad including two
statements of Peter James (dated 30th October 2025 and 4th December 2025
respectively)
xix. 16th February 2026: The Crown served an Application to Adduce Additional
Evidence and uploaded a Notice of Additional Evidence to the iPad which
contained the following: S300 of Barry Greenwood dated 16th February 2026
and BG001 and BG002 (being LED Project Calculations for the airports at
Providenciales and Grand Turk).
3 Pelican (formerly Fortis) is the electricity provider for the majority of the Turks and Caicos Islands
4 The Statements in this matter are numbered S296, S287A etc. for ease of reference
10

14. To this chronology, I add that on 26th February 2026, the Crown made a formal
application to adduce additional evidence, namely a statement of Nicquel Garland on
the grounds that:
a. Ms. Garland’s evidence formally completes the chain of custody on the
Production Order material as Peter James has already detailed in his
statements that he provided.
b. She also exhibits the letter she prepared which was served as PHJ 107(8) in the
4th December formal service.
15. It is also worthy of noting at this point that at a pre-trial hearing on 20th November,
2025, the Court had given the following Orders:
a. Crown to write to the Defence by 27th November 2025 outlining witnesses to
be called, witnesses not to be relied on, statements proposed to be read, as well
as a draft Opening Note and live versions of the redacted statements.
b. Trial Bundle including a Schedule of Witnesses and their Statements, a
Schedule of Witnesses and a Schedule of Exhibits to be filed and served on or
before 4th December 2025.
c. Rule 79 Certificate to be filed and served on or before 5th December 2025.
16. No indication was given on 20th November 2025 (or indeed at any time pre-trial) of the
Crown’s application for a Production Order to obtain billing information from Pelican
or that the Crown wished to call expert evidence in respect of the invoicing. As stated
in the timeline above5, the third Rule 79 Certificate was filed on 5th December 2025.
17. Reference has also been made by the Defence to Rule 45 and Rule 79 of the Criminal
Procedure Rules 2021 (as amended) (“the Criminal Procedure Rules”) as well as the
DPP’s Guidance on Disclosure (dated 1st December 2023). The Crown has, according
to the Defence, breached both.
18. They argue that since November 2023 when Robert Koenig was interviewed, it was
clear that the calculation of the LEDIG billing was a reasonable line of enquiry. Yet, in
spite of this, a Production Order for the relevant billing from electricity provider
Pelican (formerly “Fortis”) was not applied for until November 2025 and the Crown
5 Paragraphs 13 and 14 (supra)
11

continually certified disclosure complete while an obvious and central reasonable line
of enquiry remained active. In addition, the Crown should have notified the Court of
the Pelican Production Order after they had received the material and should have
also disclosed it to the Defence at their earliest opportunity.
19. It was submitted that since the start of the trial, the Crown has disclosed multiple new
items which amount to an evolution/change to the Crown’s case as well as Rule 79
Certificates that did not represent the true position, and the Defence is restricted in its
ability to cross-examine effectively, properly investigate the new material, take
instructions properly, instruct an expert (if required) and assess whether further
disclosure is required. It was pointed out that the fact that this is a digital trial means
that uploads occurred silently in that material was synced to iPads without notice and
it has been difficult to conduct an audit trail of the material. Some material appears in
numerous folders labelled as used and unused. By way of example, the Peter James
Statement appears in three different folders.
20. The Defence contend that in the Crown’s Opening by Mr. Andrew Mitchell KC on 15th
January 2026, it was proffered that the savings mechanism was inherently incapable
of honest calculation and that no expert or technical modelling was advanced in the
Opening. The Defence had prepared its case accordingly. It was pointed out that when
the case was opened, the Court and the Defence were unaware that a Production Order
had been granted in November 2025 requiring the Fortis billing which was in the
possession of the Crown in December 2025, and that the principal investigating officer,
Peter James, was in the process of creating schedules related to calculations drawn
from that material.
21. I have extracted the relevant portions of the Opening Address by Mr. Mitchell KC6:
Page 43:
…savings only as described below. At the beginning of each project LEDIG will calculate the
savings that will be achieved through the transformation to LED lighting. The base for
calculating the savings will be the 12-months electric utility bill, plus the cost of any alternative
power used for lighting that was not provided by the electric utilities, such as the generators,
6 Pages 43-45 and 111 to 112 of the transcript of the Opening Address
12

prior to the installation and activation of the LED lighting and other related energy-saving
products by LEDIG. If the installation is in a new facility without a previous electric utility
bill history, then the base will be calculated on the electricity consumption of comparable
tradition lighting products. TCIAA and LEDIG will agree and approve the LED lighting and
related products plan that would be installed in the anticipated savings from the base. Once the
LED lighting is installed and activated, LED would be paid monthly -- on a monthly basis
using the following formula:
Page 44:
Opening Address by Mr. Mitchell KC percentage of savings paid by the Airports Authority to
LEDIG, year one and two, 80% of the savings; 20% of the savings to be kept by the Airports
Authority from the base. Years three and four, 60% of the savings, 40% to be kept by the
Airports Authority from the base. Years five to ten, 50% of the savings with 50% to be kept by
the Airports Authority from the base. The price includes the cost of products, installation and
maintenance during the contract period. There is, it appears, no evidence of lighting only
bills rendered to LEDIG, or indeed any evidence of bills in respect of lighting at all.
There is no evidence that LEDIG rendered finally calculated monthly invoices
reflecting savings, nor is there any evidence of the amount of the invoices decreasing
as the contract passed from its second and fourth anniversaries. There is no evidence
of a difference in calculation for periods, for instance, where there were outages,
generators were
Page 45:
used during the period when the lights were installed and, of course, we know that
there were during the period hurricanes which cut power for considerable periods,
Irma and Maria. So, My Lord, that's an oversight of the contract, which didn't go
through the procurement process, which is our Count 5, and the apparent savings that
were alleged that were not capable of being calculated on the basis of the material
that was available. And that, of course, leads to Mr. Abrams, because Mr. Abrams had as
the Chief Financial Officer responsibility for ensuring, one would have thought, that the
Airports Authority did not pay money out willy-nilly. My Lord, therefore, what can I tell you
about these two men who worked for the Airports Authority, and Mr. Koenig? I've rehearsed
their character and their good standing, which is an important backdrop, of course, to this case,
and we submit it works both ways. For a man to reach Chief Executive Officer, and be in that
role for…
13

Page 111:
…this is clear for us to knit this together, paragraphs 70, 71, 72, 73, 74, 75. And at 76, I make
this observation: The Court might expect to see spreadsheets or calculations showing the
month-to-month consumption of electricity in relation to lighting and what, therefore, the
savings had been, and what was, therefore, to be the invoice. But the invoices were, as we will
see, one-liners. I've already referenced paragraph 77 during the course of my early summary of
this. But if we now look, please, to NDW3, paragraph 78. So I'm at paragraph 78 of the opening
and we're looking at NDW003. Your Lordship there sees the table of the payments on a month-
by-month basis to the Airports Authority as being reflective of, in the first instance, 80% of the
apparent savings on the electricity bill for the lights. My Lord, the invoices we will look at in a
little bit more detail. But the electricity provider, then Fortis, of course, now Pelican, don't and
didn't
Page 112:
itemise electrical uses by electrical equipment in its billing. They only, of course, referenced a
metre and how much electricity had been used by that metre. It isn't, therefore, possible, and
was never possible, according to Mr. Garth Higgs, to determine lighting, separate from
other categories of electricity used. And we observed at paragraph 78, it follows that it was
not possible to properly or honestly produce invoices based on savings. The then Deputy
Chairman says what he understood was that the baseline for the savings would be determined
by the monthly electricity costs prior to the new fixtures and bulbs being installed versus the
new costs post-installation. Well, there are some spreadsheets, but they don't really help
because there is no way, we respectfully submit, of anybody being able to say, this is what you're
spending on lighting, and this is what these LED lights will save you…

Reference was made to R v Bjelland9.
7 supra
8 [2001] UKPC 3, [2001] 58 WIR 446
9 [2009] 2 SCR 651
16

ANALYSIS
29. The thrust of the Defence submissions is that there have been breaches of disclosure
obligations and that the Crown’s case has materially and evidentially changed since
they opened it.
30. The Defence has contended that, due to the Crown’s approach to disclosure, it would
be an abuse of process to try the Defendants in that they would not get a fair trial.
31. In the alternative, the Defence contends that the evidence should be excluded.
32. I will first deal with the complaint of abuse of process.
THE ABUSE OF PROCESS APPLICATION
33. Proceedings can be stayed for abuse of process under either of the following two
limbs:
i. Limb 1:
Where the court arrives at the conclusion that the defendant cannot receive a fair trial.
ii. Limb 2:
Where the court arrives at the conclusion that it would be unfair for the defendant to
be tried.
34. In Reg v Derby Crown Court, Ex parte Brooks10, Sir Roger Omrod elaborated on these
limbs as follows:
“The power to stop a prosecution arises only when it is an abuse of process of the court. It may
be an abuse of process if either (a) the prosecution have manipulated or misused the process of
the court so as to deprive the defendant of a protection provided by the law or to take unfair
advantage of a technicality, or (b) on the balance of probabilities the defendant has been, or will
10 [1984] 80 Cr.App. R. 164, 168-169
17

be, prejudiced in the preparation or conduct of his defence by delay on the part of the prosecution
which is unjustifiable…The ultimate objective of this discretionary power is to ensure that there
should be a fair trial according to law, which involves fairness to both the defendant and the
prosecution.”
35. In Attorney General's Reference11 the House of Lords said the following in relation to
limb 2 said:
“…The category of cases in which it may be unfair to try a defendant of course includes cases
of bad faith, unlawfulness and executive manipulation of the kind classically illustrated by R v
Horseferry Road Magistrates' Court, Ex p Bennett [1994] 1 AC 42, but Mr Emmerson
contended that the category should not be confined to such cases. That principle may be broadly
accepted. There may well be cases (of which Darmalingum v The State [2000] 1 WLR 2303 is
an example) where the delay is of such an order, or where a prosecutor's breach of professional
duty is such (Martin v Tauranga District Court [1995] 2 NZLR 419 may be an example), as
to make it unfair that the proceedings against a defendant should continue. It would be unwise
to attempt to describe such cases in advance. They will be recognisable when they appear. Such
cases will however be very exceptional, and a stay will never be an appropriate remedy if any
lesser remedy would adequately vindicate the defendant's Convention right…”
36. There will be, no doubt, some degree of overlap in certain cases in that it is easy to
imagine situations where a defendant would not be able to get a fair trial and it would
also be unfair to try the defendant.
37. The Defence have, in these proceedings, based their arguments on the first limb, that
is to say that the Defendants would not be able to get a fair trial. Both the Court and
Crown counsel have pointed out that the Defence submissions appear to sometimes
delve into limb 2 and the Defence have asked the Court to consider this limb if it
considers it appropriate to do so.
38. I pause to mention delay since it has been alluded to in the Defence submissions. The
delay which concerns the Defence is not primarily pre-trial delay but delay in the
progress of the trial due to the actions of the Crown. I note that the Court can stay
11 (No 2 of 2001) [2004] 2 AC 72 at paragraph 25
18

proceedings for abuse of process where there has been unjustifiable delay by the
prosecution or delay which is so prejudicial to the defendant that a fair trial cannot be
held.
39. In R v S12, the Court of Appeal said that in the light of the authorities, the correct
approach for a judge to whom an application for a stay for abuse of process on the
ground of delay is made, is to bear in mind the following principles:
(i) Even where delay is unjustifiable, a permanent stay should be the exception rather than the
rule;
(ii) Where there is no fault on the part of the complainant or the prosecution, it will be very rare
for a stay to be granted;
(iii) No stay should be granted in the absence of serious prejudice to the defence so that no fair
trial can be held;
(iv) When assessing possible serious prejudice, the judge should bear in mind his or her power
to regulate the admissibility of evidence and that the trial process itself should ensure that all
relevant factual issues arising from delay will be placed before the jury for their consideration
in accordance with appropriate direction from the judge;
(v) If, having considered all these factors, a judge's assessment is that a fair trial will be possible,
a stay should not be granted.
40. The Crown has posited that the evidence is relevant and that the prejudicial effect is
outweighed by the probative value. An adjournment, according to the Crown, would
be a sufficient remedy to remove any prejudice accrued to the Defence.
41. In order to obtain a stay, the onus is on the Defence to establish prejudice which cannot
be cured by the granting of an adjournment or some other remedy such as the
exclusion of evidence or, where applicable, a warning to the jury.
42. I turn now to consider the evidence which the Crown is seeking to admit and examine
whether the matters complained of constitute an abuse of process and warrant a stay
of the proceedings or whether any prejudice can be cured by an adjournment or some
other remedy. While I will be examining the statements individually, I bear in mind
12 [2006] EWCA Crim 756 at paragraph 21
19

the Defence arguments that the cumulative effect of the belated disclosures and other
matters would result in the Defendants not having a fair trial.
i. Statement of Patrick Woodham
The primary Defence claim is that while this statement in itself it is not prejudicial, it
is part and parcel of the Crown’s overall approach to disclosure which is being
complained about. The statement relates to employment contracts of the First
Defendant and there seems to be no dispute about this Defendant’s employment
record. I see no prejudice to the First Defendant in this evidence being allowed.
ii. Statement of Stephen Snow
This statement affects the Second and Third Defendants and seeks to tender 26 emails
which were part of the Unused Materials and which were provided to the Defence in
June 2025. The Crown considers this to be, in the words of Crown counsel, ‘a strand of
circumstantial evidence’. I note that these materials were available to the Defence well
before the trial and do not raise any new issues. I am of the view that any prejudice
caused to the Second and Third Defendants by the belated introduction of this material
to the trial can be cured by a short adjournment so that it can be properly considered
by the Defence.
iii. Statements of Peter James
The Crown contends as follows:
Statement 287A
Statement 287A (which comprises of ten pages) of Peter James is an update to his
Statement 287 (which comprises of four pages). The additional six pages relate to
invoice materials from Fortis/Pelican. These materials are not in dispute and I see no
prejudice to the Defence in this evidence being allowed.
20

Statement 290A
This statement corrected some errors in Statement 290 which dealt with an analysis of
LEDIG’s bank account at Citibank. It was served on the Defence on 4th December 2025.
I see no prejudice in this evidence being allowed.
Statement 292
This statement produces invoices arising from a keyword of email from the Third
Defendant and the Second Defendant regarding invoices from LEDIG to TCIAA. This
material had been available to the Defence for many months.
It formally exhibits PHJ106 which was disclosed to the Defence in the trial papers on
4th December 2025.
These materials were available to the Defence before the trial and do not raise any new
issues. Any prejudice caused to the Second and Third Defendants can be cured by a
short adjournment.
iv. Statement of Malcolm Devereaux
I see nothing prejudicial to the Defence in this witness’ evidence which cannot be
addressed by an adjournment.
43. I propose to now examine the possible prejudice caused by the statements of Graham
Devine, Barry Greenwood and Nicquel Garland.
Statements of Graham Devine, Barry Greenwood and Nicquel Garland
44. In respect of Graham Devine, I pause to note that, by the agreement of the parties, this
witness was permitted to be present in Court during the proceedings in order to assist
the Crown (and by extension the Court and the Defence) with navigating the
voluminous documentation and placing them on the court screens for viewing during
the examination of witnesses. Prior to his statement which was the subject of this
application, the evidence which he was to give had been agreed by the parties. On 19th
21

January 2026, Mr. Smith KC indicated that he was withdrawing his agreement to Mr.
Devine being present in Court and subsequently filed a formal application to this
effect. The Crown, while initially suggesting that it would oppose the application,
agreed to have Mr. Devine replaced by another person. However, by this time, Mr.
Devine was already present in court when two Crown witnesses had given evidence
and their cross-examination had commenced.
45. Much of the arguments centered around this witness and my findings here will
directly impact my findings on Barry Greenwood.
46. I will, for ease of reference, repeat the arguments for the Crown in respect of the
statement of Graham Devine, which are as follows:
a. The Third Defendant’s legal team (led by Mr. Lynch KC) had prepared an
analysis of the data from the invoices on 23rd January 2026 in the form of a line
graph.
b. The Crown asked Graham Devine about the accuracy of the line graph and
Graham Devine offered to prepare his own schedule.
c. This statement explains his work on the spreadsheets in which he number
crunched the LEDIG invoices in relation to the bulbs that he could compare as
like for like to produce the spreadsheets.
d. The material was all available to the defence and was so before the moratorium.
e. According to the Crown, all that Graham Devine has done is identify, by using
the Third Defendant’s own numbers, from his invoices and projection sheet,
that the TCIAA paid more than they should have resulting from the proposed
savings to LEDIG.
47. The Crown has further contended that there is no need for the Defence to retain an
expert since the relevant expertise to analyse the spreadsheets could be provided by
the Third Defendant himself.
48. The Defence vehemently deny that the Devine spreadsheets resulted from Mr. Lynch’s
line graph and point out that the invoices could easily have been utilised by the Crown
to generate the information well before the trial. As previously stated, the Defence also
22

point to what they say is a material change in the Crown’s case as a result of the
spreadsheets (when compared with what Mr. Mitchell KC had outlined in his Opening
Address), and the prejudice in expert evidence being disclosed for the first time during
a trial. As Mr. Smith KC, pointed out in his submission, at no point during any pre-
trial hearing did the Crown indicate that it would be relying on the evidence of an
expert.
49. I have considered the arguments of the Crown and the Defence and find, in particular,
the potential for prejudice to the Defendants, particularly in the statements of Graeme
Devine, Barry Greenwood and Nicquel Garland. I say so for the following reasons:
a. The introduction of overcharging as a plank of the Crown’s case
notwithstanding the assertion of Crown counsel that the Crown’s case
essentially remains the same, being that the invoicing process was a sham.
Even if one were to agree with Crown counsel on this point, it cannot be
disputed that the specific issue of overcharging was not raised in the Crown’s
Opening. I note also that the information that was utilised to create the
spreadsheets could have been obtained at least two years before, but some of
what could have been used (being the bills) were not pursued until the
November 2025 application for a Production Order and were not obtained until
December 2025 and disclosed in January 2026. No proper explanation has been
forthcoming for this delay and it was clear from the wording of the application
for the Production Order that the Crown was hoping to rely on the fruits of the
Production Order. Notwithstanding this, the fact of the application for a
Production Order was unknown to the Defence at the time the Crown
proceeded to open its case. The Defence have therefore, during the course of
the trial, been provided with the billing information from Fortis which
conceivably could have been utilised by them to assist an expert in
understanding and possibly challenging the Devine spreadsheets and the
expert evidence of Barry Greenwood13. This billing information was pursued
by the Crown for the first time in November 2025. The Crown has not advanced
13 This is of course provided that the trial proceeds and Mr. Greenwood is deemed an expert if his evidence is allowed. Mr.
Lynch KC has indicated, as an aside, that if the matter advances to that stage, the Defence will be resisting any Crown
application to have Mr. Greenwood deemed an expert.
23

any explanation as to why this was not requested since 2021. At one stage
during the oral submissions, in an attempt to explain this delay, Crown counsel
submitted that the Production Order was sought to obtain materials which
may be of assistance to the Defence and that the Crown never intended to rely
on the bills. However, when one examines the application for the Production
Order, its contents contradict this assertion and it is clear from the wording
that the Crown was seeking materials which could assist its case.
b. I do not accept the Crown’s submission that this new spreadsheet evidence
arose from Mr. Lynch’s provision of a line graph to the Crown. Mr. Lynch’s
line graph was a simple one-page document that dealt with the Grand Turk
Airport only, with various billing dates on the x axis and kWh usage on the y
axis. In contrast, the spreadsheets produced by the Crown deal with the
airports at Grand Turk and Providenciales separately in what appear to be
extensive and complex calculations in tabular form, with each spreadsheet
comprising almost twenty rows and dozens of columns and both utilising
invoices that were available to the Crown well before the trial. They also
purport to show that there were no savings derived from the installation of the
LED lighting at the airport, a position at odds with Mr. Mitchell’s Opening, in
which he clearly stated that any savings could not be calculated.
c. As Mr. Smith KC has been at pains to point out, the presence of the witness
Graham Devine in court during proceedings, especially during the cross-
examination of Mr. Burton by Mr. Lynch KC with the resulting possible
contamination of Mr. Devine’s new evidence.
d. The need for the Defence to retain an expert to properly understand and cross-
examine Barry Greenwood and, if the need arose, counter his evidence. This
would require a fairly lengthy adjournment, resulting in a further delay to an
already protracted matter. It may also result in the unavailability of at least one
defence counsel due to other commitments which had been previously
conveyed to the Court and the parties. Apart from incurring additional legal
costs, the Defendants would now be tasked with funding the costs of this
expert. I should add that I do not consider it reasonable to expect that the Third
24

Defendant would possess the necessary expertise to analyse the spreadsheets
and provide the necessary expertise for the Defence to cross-examine Mr.
Devine and Mr. Greenwood. I am also concerned that if the Third Defendant
is tasked with providing that expertise and should the matter progress to a
stage where he is called upon to answer a case, it might infringe on his right to
silence in that he may feel obligated to provide testimony in the matter. I
therefore do not consider it to be in any way unreasonable that, if the evidence
of Barry Greenwood is allowed, the Defence would wish to retain an expert of
their own to examine and possibly challenge it.
e. By the time the Crown did in fact apply for the bills from Pelican by way of
Production Order, the bills for Providenciales Airport which immediately
preceded the introduction of the LED lighting system were unavailable, a fact
which would undoubtedly affect any comparison so as to determine savings.
50. Having so found, I must then ask myself whether there are measures which can be
adopted to cure the prejudice (such as an adjournment or the exclusion of evidence)
or, if not, whether the continued prosecution of the Defendants would result in an
abuse of process in that the Defendants would not be able to obtain a fair trial, in which
case the proceedings should be stayed.
51. The court must also factor in the effect on judicial time, a point also emphasised by Mr.
Smith KC14. This is a matter which the court has prioritised with an initial estimated
conclusion of the evidence of before Easter 2026, and any further lengthy adjournment
will likely result in the displacement of other matters which the court has scheduled
to proceed.
52. The Crown has submitted that any prejudice accruing to the Defendants as a result of
these matters can be cured by an adjournment. They have also said that in order to
assist with this, the relevant witnesses can be called as the final Crown witnesses,
thereby allowing the Defence sufficient time to prepare.
14 referencing the case of R v Boardman [2015] EWCA Crim 175
25

53. I find the Crown’s reliance on the decision of the Privy Council in Ferguson v Attorney
General of Trinidad and Tobago15 to be misplaced. In Ferguson v Attorney General of
Trinidad and Tobago, the Privy Council emphasized that where there was late
disclosure in breach of the common law duty to disclose, a realistic assessment of the
whole of the proceedings had to be undertaken to determine whether there was a
breach of the Defendant’s constitutional guarantees of due process, protection of the
law and a fair hearing. In that case, it was not alleged that the presentation of the
defence was in any way hampered by the late disclosure.16 In contrast, in the instant
case, the thrust of the Defendants’ submissions is that they would be severely
hampered in the presentation of their defence if the evidence is allowed.
54. It is, to me, not enough for the Crown to say that calling these witnesses at the end of
their case will afford the Defence with sufficient time to prepare.
55. It is clear that the Crown is presenting Mr. Greenwood as an expert.
56. In those circumstances, it would be unreasonable for even experienced counsel to be
expected to properly analyse the spreadsheets in order to be in a position to cross-
examine Mr. Devine and Mr. Greenwood (provided of course that Mr. Greenwood
satisfies the Court that he should be deemed an expert). The Defence will likely require
the retention of their own expert(s) and it would be unreasonable to expect the Defence
to retain an expert (or experts) in the middle of a trial and get instructions from the
expert(s) whilst also focusing on cross-examining witnesses and other issues in what
is a case involving voluminous documentation. The Crown’s point also ignores that
the Defence might require the information from their experts to cross-examine other
witnesses called before Mr. Devine and Mr. Greenwood.
57. In considering whether to continue with the proceedings or whether the proceedings
would amount to an abuse of process and a stay of proceedings should be granted, I
must ask myself whether there are alternatives that can be explored. There are two
possible alternatives in this matter:
15 (2001) 58 WIR 446
16 See paragraphs 15 to 18 of the judgment.
26

a. The granting of an adjournment to provide the defence with an opportunity to
properly examine and take instructions on the newly disclosed material. This
would allow the Defence sufficient time to retain and obtain instructions from
their own expert(s).
b. The exclusion of evidence which can be considered to be prejudicial.
58. However, in respect of the statements of Graeme Devine, Barry Greenwood and, to a
lesser extent, Nicquel Garland, the cumulative effect of the matters outlined at
paragraph 4917 make an adjournment not a viable option. Indeed, no adjournment can
cure the potential prejudice to the Defendants of Mr. Devine’s being in Court while a
witness was being cross-examined on matters which directly relate to the subsequent
statement and spreadsheets that he produced.
59. In respect of the other witnesses18, however, as I have already stated, I find that any
prejudice caused by the lateness of the disclosure can be cured by the granting of a
short adjournment if the Defence require it.
60. I must then explore the other option available to the Court: the exclusion of the
evidence of the statements of Graeme Devine, Barry Greenwood and Nicquel Garland.
For the reasons which I have gone through, I consider this to be a viable option, given
the potentially prejudicial nature of the evidence, and in particular the spreadsheets.
The Crown had commenced its case without this evidence and I see no reason why the
Crown cannot complete its case without it. I find that if excluded, the prejudice can be
eliminated and the Defendants can have a fair trial. It would also address the issue of
delay which can be occasioned by a lengthy adjournment to retain an expert to
properly consider the evidence from the spreadsheets and study the billing
information in order to potentially challenge the conclusions of Mr. Devine and Mr.
Greenwood.
61. In respect of limb 1, I therefore find that the prejudice accrued to the Defence by the
statements of Graeme Devine, Barry Greenwood and Nicquel Garland can be cured
by the exclusion of their statements and, as such, I find no justification for the matter
17 supra
18 Being Patrick Woodham, Stephen Snow, Peter James and Malcolm Devereaux
27

to be stayed. I acknowledge that I find the statement of Nicquel Garland to be the least
troubling of the three. However, as I have canvassed later in my analysis of the powers
that can be exercised by the Court under the Criminal Proceedings Rules, I have also
excluded it for more compelling reasons.
62. In respect of the other statements, the granting of an adjournment can cure any
prejudice. Once these measures are adopted, I find that it would not be an abuse of
process under the first limb to continue the trial against the Defendants. The
application to stay proceedings on the basis of abuse of process under limb 1 therefore
fails.
LIMB 2
63. Before concluding, I consider it necessarily to address limb 2 briefly because having
found that, with the exclusion of certain evidence, the Defendant can get a fair trial, I
can still stay the proceedings on the basis of abuse of process if I find that it would be
unfair to try the Defendants.
64. In R v Horseferry Road Magistrate Court, Ex p Bennett19, Lord Lowry said20:
“…the court, in order to protect its own process from being degraded and misused, must have
the power to stay proceedings which have come before it and have only been made possible by
acts which offend the court’s conscience as being contrary to the rule of law. Those acts by
providing a morally unacceptable foundation for the exercise of jurisdiction over the suspect
taint the proposed trial and, if tolerated, will mean that the court’s process has been
abused…” [Emphasis mine].
65. In R v Latif21, Lord Steyn said22 in relation to limb 2:
“The law is settled. Weighing countervailing considerations of policy and justice, it is for the
judge in the exercise of his discretion to decide whether there has been an abuse of process, which
amounts to an afront to the public conscience and requires the criminal proceedings to be
19 [1994] 1 AC 42
20 at p 76C—D
21 [1996] 1 WLR 104, 112F)
22 at 112-113
28

stayed: Reg v Horseferry Road Magistrates Court, Ex p Bennett [1994] 1 AC 42. Ex p Bennett
was a case where a stay was appropriate because a defendant had been forcibly abducted and
brought to this country to face trial in disregard of extradition laws. The speeches in Ex p
Bennett conclusively establish that proceedings may be stayed in the exercise of the judge s
discretion not only where a fair trial is impossible but also where it would be contrary to the
public interest in the integrity of the criminal justice system that a trial should take place. An
infinite variety of cases could arise. General guidance as to how the discretion should be
exercised in particular circumstances will not be useful. But it is possible to say that in a case
such as the present the judge must weigh in the balance the public interest in ensuring that
those that are charged with grave crimes should be tried and the competing public interest in
not conveying the impression that the court will adopt the approach that the end justifies any
means.”
66. Whilst the actions of the Crown in respect of the manner in which disclosure was
effected and the other acts complained of cannot be applauded, I am not of the view
that they cross the threshold so as to offend the court’s conscience as being contrary to
the rule of law or to amount to an affront to the public conscience, or that it would be
contrary to the public interest in the integrity of the criminal justice system for a trial
to take place.
67. I see no mala fides in the part of the Crown, and the Defence have, to their credit,
acknowledged this. In the circumstances, I see no reason for the proceedings to be
stayed as an abuse of process under limb 2.
Breach of the Disclosure Orders
68. Notwithstanding my conclusion to exclude the statements of Graeme Devine, Barry
Greenwood and Nicquel Garland, I still propose to consider the effect of breaches of
the Disclosure Order.
69. At common law, the court enjoys the discretion to exclude prosecution evidence if the
prejudicial effect outweighs its probative value23.
23
See: Selvey v DPP [1970] AC 304, 52 Cr App Rep 443, HL; R v Sang [1980] AC 402, 69 Cr App Rep 282.
29

70. Reference has been made by the Crown to R v Bjelland24, a decision of the Supreme
Court of Canada, where the trial judge had ordered the exclusion of evidence which
had only been disclosed in March and April of 2006 with the trial set to commence on
1st May 2006. The evidence was excluded under section 24 of the Canadian Charter of
Rights and Freedoms. The Court of Appeal set aside the acquittal and ordered a new
trial on the ground that the judge had failed to consider whether a less severe remedy
than exclusion of evidence could have cured the prejudice to the Defendant. The
Accused appealed to the Supreme Court of Canada and the appeal was dismissed, and
it was held, inter alia, that exclusion of evidence obtained in conformity with the
Charter is only available as a remedy under section 24 where the late disclosure
renders the trial process unfair and this unfairness cannot be remedied by way of an
adjournment. It is noteworthy that in Bjelland, the late disclosure had been provided
in the two months preceding the trial and not during the trial.
71. The probative value of the evidence of Graeme Devine, Barry Greenwood and Nicquel
Garland to the Crown, as I understand it, is to counter Mr. Lynch’s line graph and to
particularise what the Crown contends was an invoicing sham.
72. By way of summary, the matters of prejudice can be succinctly put as follows:
a. The introduction of overcharging as a plank of the Crown’s case
notwithstanding the assertion of Crown counsel that the Crown’s case
essentially remains the same, being that the invoicing process was a sham.
Even if one were to agree with Crown counsel on this point, it cannot be
disputed that the specific issue of overcharging was not raised in the Crown’s
Opening.
b. The presence of the witness Graeme Devine in court during proceedings,
especially during the cross-examination of Mr. Burton by Mr. Lynch KC with
the resulting possible contamination of Mr. Devine’s new evidence.
24 [2009] 2 S.C.R. 651
30

c. The need for the Defence to retain an expert to properly understand and, if the
need arose, counter the evidence of Barry Greenwood. This would require a
fairly lengthy adjournment, resulting in a further delay to an already
protracted matter. It may also result in the unavailability of at least one defence
counsel. In addition to additional legal costs, the Defendants would now be
tasked with funding the costs of this expert.
d. By the time the Crown did in fact apply for the bills by way of Production
Order, the bills for Providenciales Airport which immediately preceded the
introduction of the LED lighting system were unavailable, a fact which would
undoubtedly affect any comparison so as to determine savings.
e. The effect on judicial time.
73. For the same concerns that I had expressed during my analysis of the abuse of process
application, I consider the prejudicial effect of the statements of Graeme Devine, Barry
Greenwood and Nicquel Garland to outweigh the probative value and that this
prejudice cannot be remedied by an adjournment. I do not however form the same
view in respect of the other statements.
74. At common law therefore, I would also exclude the statements of Graeme Devine,
Barry Greenwood and Nicquel Garland.
75. I move on to consider the position under the Criminal Procedure Rules 2021 (as
amended). Rule 79 was amended in December 2025 to insert subsections 5, 6 and 7
(erroneously labelled ‘6’).
76. This requires separate consideration since, apart from my consideration of whether to
stay proceedings as an abuse of process, the December 2025 amendment to Rule 79
(and more particularly the newly inserted Rule 79(7) which was erroneously published
as a second Rule 79(6)) now empowers the Court to dismiss the charges against the
Defendant as a sanction where a prosecutor fails to comply with the order of the court
for disclosure:
Rule 79 of the principal Rules is amended by inserting the following subrules after subrule (4)—
31

“(5) The prosecution shall provide a separate schedule of unused materials as part of its disclosure
requirements in respect of unused materials in subrule (1).
(6) The Court may, in its discretion, impose one or more of the following sanctions where a
prosecutor is found to have failed to disclose exculpatory evidence—
(a) hold the prosecutor in contempt of court;
(b) dismiss the charge;
(c) acquit the defendant;
(d) disallow the prosecution from leading or otherwise using at trial any evidence not disclosed
unless the evidence will be of assistance to the defence; or
(e) where the matter is the subject of an appeal, overturn a conviction or grant a new trial.
(6) The Court may, in its discretion, impose one or more of the following sanctions where a
prosecutor fails to comply with the order of the court for disclosure—
(a) dismiss the charge; or
(b) disallow the prosecution from leading or otherwise using at trial any evidence not
disclosed unless it will be of assistance to the defence. [Emphasis mine].
77. The initial Rule 79 read as follows:
(1) The prosecutor must disclose to the accused any prosecution material used or unused which has not
previously been disclosed to the accused.
(2) The prosecutor must disclose evidence which may be exculpatory or in the opinion of the
prosecutor might undermine the case for the prosecution against the accused unless the judge
excludes such material in the public interest.
(3) Give to the accused a written statement that there is no material of a description mentioned in
subrule (2).
(4) A defendant or his Attorney may make an application to the Court to permit the defendant to
inspect and copy relevant prosecution material if not made available under subrule (1) or (2).
78. I pause at this point to note the overriding objectives of the Rules25:
(1) The overriding objective of these Rules is to ensure that criminal cases are dealt with justly.
(2) It is the duty of the court and all parties and participants, at every stage of the proceedings to
25 As outlined in Rule 4
32

advance the overriding objective.
(3) Dealing with a criminal case justly includes—
(a) dealing with a case efficiently and expeditiously;
(b) dealing with the prosecution and the defence fairly;
(c) considering the interests of the parties, witnesses, victims and jurors and keeping them
informed of the progress of the case as necessary;
(d) ensuring the protection of the rights of a defendant;
(e) ensuring that appropriate information is available to the court particularly when bail and
sentence are under consideration; and
(f) dealing with a case in a way that takes into account—
(i) the gravity of the offence;
(ii) the complexity of the issues;
(iii) the consequences for the defendant and others affected;
(iv) the needs of other cases; and
(iv) allotting a case an appropriate share of the court’s resources, while taking into
account the need of resources to other cases.
(4) The court must seek to give effect to the overriding objective when the court—
(a) exercises any discretion given to it by these Rules; or
(b) interprets the meaning of any Ordinance, rule or practice direction.
(5) Each party shall actively assist the court in fulfilling its duty under subrule (4) whether or not the
court has given a direction; and may apply for a direction if needed.
(6) It is the duty of the parties and participants in a criminal case to—
(a) prepare and conduct the case in accordance with the overriding objective;
(b) comply with the rules, any practice directions, orders and directions made or given by the
court;
(c) immediately inform the court and all parties of—
(i) any significant failure (whether or not that party or participant is responsible for
the
failure) to take any procedural step required by these Rules, a practice direction or any
order or direction made by the court;
(ii) anything that may affect the date or duration of the trial;
(iii) anything that may significantly affect the progress of the case in any other way.
79. It is clear that the objective of Rule 79 and the December 2025 amendment to Rule 79
was to to place obligations on the Crown in respect of disclosure, together with
33

accompanying sanctions for failure to comply. The court is given a discretion in the
event of the failure of the Crown to comply with any order in respect of disclosure.
This discretion includes the power to dismiss the matter or to exclude any evidence.
Needless to say, the court will only dismiss the matter in exceptional circumstances
where the court forms the view that any other remedy, such as the exclusion of
evidence or the granting of an adjournment in order to afford the defence an
opportunity to deal with the newly disclosed material, will not suffice. I pause to make
a general point that the exercise of the Court’s discretion to dismiss the charges under
Rule 79(7) will not be a bar to future prosecutions and a Defendant will be unable to
successfully plead autrefois acquit. However, one can envisage an application to stay
proceedings for abuse of process in certain instances if fresh charges on the same
evidence are preferred, depending on the stage that the initial matter was at when it
was dismissed. Whether such an application will succeed will of course depend on the
particular circumstances of that case.
80. Before moving on, I add that I am in agreement with the Defence that the Second
Defendant’s exclusion application does not require the Court to apply delegated
legislation retroactively but rather to ask the Court to consider the history of the case
in its application of Rule 79 of the Criminal Procedure Rules.
81. The court in exercising its discretion under Rule 79(7) will have as its paramount
consideration what would be in the best interests of justice, including any fairness to
the defendant and the Crown.
82. Disclosure is a critical part of the criminal trial process. Its rational is simple: the
defendant must be aware of the case that he has to meet in order to properly instruct
his attorney-at-law, if he desires to retain one, and to mount a defence to the charge.
The Defendant must also be provided with materials in the possession of the Crown
that may either exculpate him or assist him in mounting a defence.
83. Failure to disclose items or matters can even lead to the quashing of a conviction on
appeal26.
26 See for example Glenroy Bishop v The State (2000) 60 WIR 370
34

84. It is incumbent on the Crown to ensure that all material in its possession which can
exculpate or be of assistance to the Defendant in his case is disclosed in a timely
manner so as to afford the defendant with the sufficient opportunity to utilise the
materials to properly prepare his defence. In addition, material which the Crown is
relying on must be provided to the Defence in a timely manner so that the Defendant
knows the case that he has to meet. It is for this reason that pre-trial directions are
given for disclosure.
85. It is not in dispute that the Crown has filed Rule 79 certificates on three occasions but
has proceeded to furnish disclosure after these filings.
86. It is also not in dispute that the Crown has breached the Order of the court by
providing materials to the Defence after the most recent deadline for disclosure.
87. In his submission, Mr. Lynch KC suggested a three-stage approach in dealing with
breaches of the Court’s Order:
a. How flagrant is the breach?
b. Has it been repeated?
c. Is the effect to create a sense of unfairness to the Accused?
88. I am more or less in agreement with Mr. Lynch KC, but wish to add that there are
instances where a single breach of an Order might be sufficient to attract the ultimate
sanction of dismissal of a case. Arguably, one such situation might be where an Order
is made during a trial and the Crown is in flagrant breach of it. The single breach of
such an Order at such an advanced stage in the proceedings may, in certain
circumstances create such unfairness to the Defendant that the proceedings cannot be
salvaged.
89. In determining what if any sanction should be imposed under Rule 79(7), it may be
helpful for the Court to ask itself the following questions:
a. Has the Order been breached by the Crown?
b. Is this the first breach of the Court’s Order in respect of disclosure or have there
been repeated breaches?
c. At what stage of the matter has the breach occurred?
35

d. Is there a reasonable explanation for the breach of the Court’s Order?
e. Has the breach resulted in prejudice to the Defendant?
f. If the answer to (e) is yes, would it be fair to the Defendant for the matter to
proceed or, if applicable, for the newly disclosed evidence to be allowed?
90. In the instant case, there have been repeated and flagrant breaches of disclosure Orders
by the Crown, as detailed without challenge in the James Shepherd Notes27. I have
already outlined the prejudice accrued to the Defendants in my analysis under the
common law and I rely on the same matters in respect of that here in respect of the
statements of Graeme Devine, Barry Greenwood and Nicquel Garland as resulting in
unfairness to the Defendants. I do not consider the reasons advanced by the Crown to
be reasonable. Indeed, I consider the lateness of the application in respect of the
witness Nicquel Garland to be particularly inexplicable, it having been made on 26th
February 2026 and being in respect of a Production Order which was applied for in
November 2025.
91. I must now ask myself, independent of my finding on the abuse of process application,
whether a sanction should be imposed and if so, what sanction.
92. Having regard to the procedural history of the matter, the timing of the breaches and
the general approach of the Crown to disclosure I consider the breaches to be serious
and are worthy of sanction. In this regard, there are two options: dismissing the
charges or excluding the evidence.
93. Only two witnesses have given evidence thus far and the Defence has reserved the
right to recall them for cross-examination. I form the view that to exclude the evidence
of Graham Devine, Barry Greenwood and Nicquel Garland will remove any
unfairness to the Defendant and, in these circumstances, be an adequate sanction for
the Crown’s breach of Rule 79(7).
94. As such, my conclusions in respect of the abuse of process application, the applications
to adduce additional evidence (which was countered by the application to exclude
evidence filed by the second Defendant and adopted by all Defendants), and the
exercise of my powers under Rule 79(7) are the same.
27 See paragraph 16 (supra)
36

The Presentation of the Disclosure
95. Having excluded the statements of Graeme Devine, Barry Greenwood and Nicquel
Garland, I must go further in order to ensure that the Defendants can have a fair trial.
96. The Court remains extremely concerned by the disorganised manner in which
disclosure has been provided by the Crown.
97. The offences on the indictment are part of a series of offences which were separated
into different indictments, and will therefore be the subject of different trials. This is
the first of these indictments and the first such trial. It is noteworthy that the Third
Defendant is only before the court in one of these indictments.
98. In his submission on 24th February 2026, junior counsel for the First Named Defendant
Mr. James Shepherd outlined what he submitted was a plethora of failures by the
Crown to fulfil its disclosure and other obligations. In identifying what he described
as the fourteenth failure, Mr. Shepherd submitted, inter alia, that in respect to
disclosure, the Defence still do not have an Unused Materials Disclosure Schedule
relevant to this indictment. Instead, the Unused Materials schedule provided by the
Crown to the Defence relates to all indictments. Mr. Shepherd then went on to draw
reference to the Third Named Defendant, who is only concerned with this indictment.
The Crown has not taken issue with Mr. Shepherd’s submission in this regard.
99. I find this state of affairs to be alarming, especially if this is indeed still the state of
affairs. I remind of Rule 79(5) of the Rules which provides as follows:
“(5) The prosecution shall provide a separate schedule of unused materials as part of its
disclosure requirements in respect of unused materials in subrule (1).”
100. It is therefore incumbent on the Crown to provide a separate schedule of
unused materials as part of its disclosure requirements.
101. I note that we are still early days in the trial with only two witnesses having
started their evidence. In the circumstances, I order that the Crown is to, on or before
37

Tuesday 11th March 2026 at 4:30 p.m., provide the Defence with complete schedules
clearly identifying the documents and other materials which are being relied on in this
trial and clearly identifying what will be unused materials in this trial.
102. The Court recognizes the constraints of counsel for the Crown and the
shortages of staff and resources at the Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions.
Nothing in this ruling should be taken as apportioning personal blame to any of the
counsel for the Crown for the issues which the Court has raised. The Court is cognizant
that Ms. Grant has come on record for the Crown late last year and that Mr. Mitchell
KC has been on and off record, and that they have been provided with limited support
in a matter with voluminous documentation involving multiple defendants.
Nevertheless, the lack of resources in the Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions
is no fault of the Defendants and cannot be used as a reason for the failure to provide
disclosure in a timely and efficient manner.
103. I form the view that the exclusion of the statements of Graeme Devine, Barry
Greenwood and Nicquel Garland coupled with the Order I intend to make regarding
the provision by the Crown to the Defence of updated schedules of disclosure will
adequately address the Defence complaints, which were succinctly summarised in
their submission as: Disclosure failures, potential delay, evidential evolution and what
they describe as the ‘mid-trial reformulation of the prosecution case”, and that,
together with a short adjournment to consider the Crown’s Schedules, will sufficiently
address any potential prejudice which can be accrued to the Defence in answering the
counts on the indictment.
The Order of the Court is therefore as follows:
1. The Defence application to stay proceedings on the ground of abuse of process fails.
2. In respect of the Crown’s application to adduce additional evidence and the Defence
application to exclude evidence:
a. The Court will not exclude the statement of Patrick Woodham.
38

b. The Court will not exclude the Statement of Stephen Snow.
c. In respect of the statements of Peter James:
i. The Court will not exclude Statement 287A.
ii. The Court will not exclude Statement 290A.
iii. The Court will not exclude Statement 292.
d. The Statement of Graeme Devine is hereby excluded.
e. The Statement of Barry Greenwood is hereby excluded.
f. The Statement of Nicquel Garland is hereby excluded.
3. The Crown shall provide the Defence with complete schedules clearly identifying the
documents and other materials which are being relied on in this trial and clearly
identifying what will be unused materials on or before Tuesday 11th March 2026 at 4:30
p.m.
4. The Court will not exclude the statement of Malcolm Devereaux.
Dated 16th March, 2026
The Honourable Justice Chris Selochan
Judge of the Supreme Court
39
court: Supreme Court of Turks and Caicos Islands
summary: Late Crown disclosure did not justify a stay, but key spreadsheet/expert evidence was excluded for prejudicial late disclosure.

title: Cedric Simms v The Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands (CL G-CL 5/2022) [2026] TCASC 16 (27 February 2026)
text:
IN THE SUPREME COURT ACTION NO. CL G-CL 5/22
TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
BETWEEN:
CEDRIC SIMMS
PLAINTIFF
AND
THE ATTORNEY GENERAL OF THE TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
DEFENDANT
__________________________________________________________________
DECISION
__________________________________________________________________
Before His Lordship Justice Chris Selochan.
Appearances: Mr. Noel Skippings for the Plaintiff
Ms. Clemar Hippolyte and Ms. Khadija McFarlane for the
Defendant
1

Hearing Dates: 6th and 7th November 2025
Venue: Court Room #1, Supreme Court, Providenciales and virtually from the
Supreme Court, Grand Turk
Handed Down: 17th December, 2025
BACKGROUND
1. By Statement of Claim filed on 24th October, 2022, the Plaintiff seeks Damages (including
Aggravated Damages) for False Arrest, False Imprisonment and Assault and Battery as
well as costs.
2. The Plaintiff’s case is that he was unlawfully arrested by the police in Grand Turk, Turks
and Caicos Islands for breach of an Interim Protection Order contrary to section 16 of the
Domestic Violence Act CAP 11:03 (“the Domestic Violence Act”). He contends that his
arrest was unlawful because it failed to fulfil the requirements of section 22(3) of the
Domestic Violence Act CAP in that, prior to effecting the arrest, the police were not in
possession of a sworn affidavit.
3. The Plaintiff contends that following his arrest he was detained at the police station from
4th August 2022 to 10th August 2022, a period which he submits amounts to false
imprisonment. In the alternative, he claims that he was unlawfully detained from 6th
August 2022 to 10th August 2022 since, pursuant to section 22(6)(b)(i) of the Domestic
Violence Act, he should not be detained for a period of in excess of forty-eight (48) hours
before being brought before a magistrate or Justice of the Peace.
4. The Plaintiff claims that at the police station, he was placed in handcuffs and leg shackles,
including on at least two occasions during visits by his attorney-at-law. He also claims to
have suffered mental, physical and emotional pain and anguish by having to remain in
2

‘the dank, dirty, smelly prisoners’ cell’ (to use the words of the Plaintiff’s attorney-at-law
in his closing submission) at the police station.
5. In its Defence dated 2nd August 2024, the Defendant acknowledges that the Plaintiff was
indeed arrested on 4th August 2022 in Grand Turk for breach of an Interim Protection
Order. However, the Defendant contends that the arrest was lawful since the police had
reasonable grounds to suspect that the Plaintiff was in breach of an Interim Protection
Order, and that belief was based on a report from the Plaintiff’s wife that the Plaintiff had
intimidated, harassed and come within 100 feet of her at her workplace and threatened
physical violence towards her contrary to the terms of the Protection Order. The
Defendant admits that at the time of the arrest the police officers had not obtained the
requisite affidavit from the victim but contends that they had reasonable grounds to
believe that the Plaintiff had breached the Protection Order based on the information and
circumstances available to them at the time, and that on the following day (being 5th
August 2022) the Plaintiff’s wife signed a written statement evidencing the breach of the
Interim Protection Order.
6. In respect of the Plaintiff’s detention, the Defendant contends that on Friday 5th August
2022, the Plaintiff was taken to the Grand Turk Magistrate’s Court, but the court was not
sitting on that day. As such, the first available court date after the expiry of the 48-hour
period was on Monday 8th August 2022 and that, at best, any unlawful imprisonment was
for a period of two days from 8th August 2022 to 10th August 2022.
7. The Defendant admits that the Plaintiff was placed in handcuffs and shackles but
contends that this was done based on a reasonable assessment of the situation and the
perceived risk posed by the Plaintiff. According to the Defendant, these actions did not
involve excessive force and were necessary and proportionate in order to ensure the
Plaintiff’s compliance, prevent escape and to protect the officers, the Plaintiff and others,
and was only done as a security measure when the Plaintiff was taken out of the cell. The
Defendant contends that the Plaintiff was known to the law, had previously been arrested
and was considered to be at a high risk of escaping custody.
3

8. In respect of the prison conditions, the Defendant contends that the conditions of the
Plaintiff’s detention were humane and lawful and that:
a. The Plaintiff was the only person in the cell and had access to a shower and toilet
facilities while in custody.
b. The Plaintiff did in fact take showers.
c. The Plaintiff, on his request, was taken out of the cell for smoking breaks.
d. The Plaintiff was offered meals regularly.
e. The Plaintiff was given medication and escorted to the hospital to receive medical care
when requested.
f. The police checked on the Plaintiff regularly to ensure that he was in good health.
g. The Plaintiff was familiar with the detention process and had been incarcerated on
numerous previous occasions without making similar claims against the Defendant,
so it is implausible that this detention would uniquely cause mental and emotional
distress, physical pain and anguish.
9. As such, the Defendant contends that the Claim against the Defendant should be
dismissed with costs awarded to the Defendant.
10. In a Reply filed on 12th November 2024, the Plaintiff denies that he had ever shown any
propensity to be violent or to try to escape, and points out that he was at a police station
with many police officers and was in an enclosed room. For the first time, the Plaintiff
makes reference to being tasered by the police, pleading the following:
Paragraph 5: “…Furthermore, the tasering of the Plaintiff was a very painful experience and
unnecessary. It was also unlawful.”
Paragraph 8: “…The whole tasering, arrest, and detention was unlawful. Aggravated damages,
punitive damages etc. should be awarded.”
4

11. The trial of this matter was held virtually on Thursday 6th November 2025 and Friday 7th
November 2025.
12. The only witness statement filed on behalf of the Plaintiff was that of the Plaintiff himself.
13. On behalf of the Defendant, the following two police officers gave witness statements:
i. Emeial Shepherd.
ii. Hussien Higgs.
THE EVIDENCE
PLAINTIFF’S WITNESS
a. Cedric Simms
14. The only witness for the Plaintiff was the Plaintiff himself who gave a short witness
statement comprising twenty-four (24) lines that essentially repeated what was pleaded
in his Statement of Claim, with one interesting addition. He began his statement by
saying: “On the 4th August 2022 I was chased by the police in Grand Turk and tasered
unlawfully and arrested unlawfully.” (Emphasis mine).
15. He went on to state: “The assault by the police with the taser was blatantly unlawful and
contrary to the police’s policy on the use of the taser, and the arrest was unlawful and
contrary to section 22(3)(a) and (b) of the DVO 2025.”
16. The Plaintiff has therefore, in his witness statement, stated that he was tasered and is
seeking damages for it.
5

17. At the trial, the Plaintiff did not seek to amplify his witness statement prior to being cross-
examined by counsel for the Defendant, Ms. Khadija McFarlane.
18. I have extracted what I consider to be important parts of the cross-examination of the
Plaintiff in which he responds to matters which were put to him by counsel for the
Defendant:
Put: You were upset and that was the reason why on 4th August 2022 you went to her place of
work.
Answer: Not true. I went to the supermarket. My wife works at the supermarket. She has her
times. It is Sunny Foods Supermarket. On 4th August 2022 when I went there, my wife was
working at that time.
Put: You were upset and started threatening your wife at her place of work.
Answer: It is not true that I started threatening my wife at her place of work. I was not upset.
Put: In threatening her, you told her you could make her lose her job.
Answer: I know nothing about that.
Put: You ran behind her at her place of work when she tried to get away.
Answer: I do not know what you call ‘ran’. I can’t recall that.
Put: You told your wife, “I want to see you sleep tonight.”
6

Ans: I do not know nothing about that.
Put: During your interactions with your wife she indicated to you she would call the police.
A: I know nothing about that.
Put: Your response was “F.. police’
A: I know nothing about that.
19. In response to questions by Ms. McFarlane concerning his arrest and whether he had
recognized the vehicle as a police vehicle, the Plaintiff went on to say, inter alia:
“…In my witness statement that I signed in relation to this matter, I told this court that police
officers arrested me on 4th August 2022. This was the same day that I was at my wife’s place of
work. Later on that same date I was driving my vehicle. No one tried to stop me. A car stopped in
front of me. I got out of my car and ran. No one told me they were police officers. The car that
stopped me, I saw the back of the vehicle. Having seen the back of it, I took no time to look to see if
it was a marked police vehicle. It is not true that the police vehicle had on its siren. I do not know
what vehicle it was. The vehicle blocked my vehicle. After my vehicle was blocked, I ran out of my
vehicle and fled. I do not know who it was that was behind me and whether there were police
shouting at me to stop. I did not know who it was behind me. I continued running. I find out after
that there were police officers behind me telling me to stop…”
20. The Plaintiff was then questioned about the portion of his witness statement in which he
had stated that he was tasered. His responses were, inter alia:
“…I fell down. The police put his feet on my neck and tasered me. I did not put this in my witness
statement. I accept I did not put it. That don’t mean it didn’t happen…. When the police caught
up to me they did not inform me that I was arrested for breach of an interim Protection Order. I
7

know that when I reached court. They just put me in a cell. They never informed me of that. They
tasered me in my back and put me in a cell…”
DEFENCE WITNESSES
a. Emeial Shepherd
21. The first Defence witness was Emeial Shepherd, who is a sergeant of police (Number 183)
with the Royal Turks and Caicos Islands Police Force and was, at the time of the incident,
stationed in Grand Turk, Turks and Caicos Islands.
22. In his Witness Statement, he said that on Thursday 4th August 2022, at around 7:30 p.m.,
he was on duty at the Grand Turk Police Station when a report was received from one
Guilene Fils Amie Simms that her husband, Cedric Simms, was at her place of work at
Sunny Foods Supermarket located on West Road, Grand Turk, harassing her and causing
a disturbance.
23. P.C. Shepherd stated that Police Constable Higgs and Police Constable Brown responded
to the report. Sometime after, they returned to the station, following which another report
came in stating that the Plaintiff had returned to his wife’s place of work. Sgt. Shepherd
stated that he was aware that there was an Interim Protection Order issued against the
Plaintiff because it was circulated amongst officers and a copy was placed on the notice
board of the police station. A copy of the Interim Protection Order was annexed to his
Witness Statement as “E.S.1”. It is instructive that one of the terms of the order is that the
Plaintiff is not to come within 100 feet of his wife, Guilene Fils Amie Simms.
8

24. According to Sgt. Shepherd, he, in company with Officers Marissa Brown and Hussien
Higgs, proceeded to the supermarket but did not see the Plaintiff on their arrival there.
However, one of the supermarket’s employees advised him that the Plaintiff’s wife had
locked herself in an office because she was in fear of the Plaintiff. Sgt. Shepherd then went
to the office and met Mrs. Simms who told her that the Plaintiff had returned to the
supermarket after the police had left the first time. Sgt. Shepherd was then shown CCTV
footage of Mr. Simms in the supermarket.
25. Later that day, Sgt. Shepherd was driving a marked police vehicle in the vicinity of
Hospital Road, where he observed the Plaintiff driving in the same area. Sgt. Shepherd
drove the vehicle to block the path of the Plaintiff’s vehicle. The Plaintiff then exited his
vehicle and ran. Sgt Shepherd and Police Constable Bido chased the Plaintiff, with Sgt.
Shepherd shouting behind him to stop. However, the Plaintiff continued running. Sgt.
Shepherd and Police Constable Bido apprehended the Plaintiff with the use of a taser. Sgt.
Shepherd stated that the taser was used because the Plaintiff refused to stop, it was a less
lethal option and the Plaintiff is known to be aggressive towards police officers.
26. Sgt. Shepherd stated that after apprehending the Plaintiff, he cautioned him and informed
him that he was under arrest for breach of a Protection Order issued by the Court, and the
Plaintiff was subsequently charged with breach of the Interim Protection Order. He
further stated that he had arrested the Plaintiff pursuant to the powers of arrest without
warrant under section 20 of the Domestic Violence Act.
27. Whilst acknowledging that he did not have a copy of an affidavit from Mrs. Simms before
making the arrest as required by section 22(3)(b) of the Domestic Violence Act, he
contended that he did interview her at her place of employment and that in any event any
affidavit would have been sworn to by her since she was the applicant in the domestic
violence proceedings.
9

28. Sgt. Shepherd went on to describe the conditions of the cell at the Grand Turk Police
Station in which the Plaintiff was housed. According to him, the holding cell is double
bunk and houses a toilet, and there are shower facilities along the inner corridor at the
end.
29. He added the following:
i. On Friday 5th August 2022, Police Constable Hussien Higgs went to the magistrate’s
court to make arrangements for the Plaintiff’s appearance but was informed that the
magistrate was not in office on that day. Later that day, the Plaintiff was given the
opportunity to meet with his attorney-at-law at the Grand Turk Police Station.
ii. The Plaintiff had previously been charged with other offences and was considered to
be at a high risk of escaping custody, hence the reason for him being placed in
handcuffs when meeting with his attorney-at-law. A copy of the Plaintiff’s Conviction
Sheet was annexed to his Witness Statement and marked “ES2”.
iii. P.C. Higgs went to the magistrate’s court on Monday 8th August 2022 to make
arrangements for the Plaintiff to appear, but was informed that a file needed to be
produced.
iv. On Wednesday 10th August 2022, P.C. Higgs took the Plaintiff to the Grand Turk
Magistrate’s Court where he appeared before the magistrate and was remanded into
custody until 31st August 2022.
v. During the time that the Plaintiff was in custody at the Grand Turk Police Station, he
requested certain meals that were not on the menu and they were provided to him.
The relevant Movement of Prisoner Sheets for the Plaintiff (which recorded meals,
smoking breaks, visits by attorneys and other persons, removal for medical attention
and court appearances etc.) were annexed and exhibited to his Witness Statement and
marked “E.S.3”.
30. Under cross-examination by Mr. Skippings, P.C. Shepherd’s responses included:
10

“…I was part of the process of when Mr. Simms was arrested. I did not taser him. I know who did
it. P.C. Lewis Bido did it. I was present at the time. When Mr. Simms was tasered, he was nowhere
near to the supermarket. A taser should be used by the police on someone in order to effect an arrest
where use of force is needed. I am aware of the Use of Force Police Policy about using the taser. I
am not aware that para 9.1 of the policy says a taser should only be used where there is violence or
threat of violence. I would have to look back at that policy…
…I was present when he was tasered. Just before he was tasered, he was escaping custody. He was
running. The taser was used in order to subdue him. He was tasered once. The taser is equipped
with two cartridges. He was tasered. He fell to the ground. Before he was tasered, we shouted to
him to stop by saying: “Stop! Stop!” I shouted nothing else. I shouted “Stop!” from the distance I
was. There was a 25 feet distance of him from me. The one who tasered him was closer to him. He
was running. I cannot recall everything that was said. Once he was tasered, P.C. Bido placed the
handcuffs on him and stand him up. We held him on the ground in order to put the handcuffs on.
I never placed my leg on his neck. He was laying on his back. We held his lower leg and arm and
placed the handcuffs on him. I cannot recall what part of the body P.C. Bido held.
I just gave instructions about what they should do. I gave them instructions to take him before the
magistrate’s court. He was supposed to go to court the following day. However the magistrate was
not there. We went to the magistrate’s court in Grand Turk. I spoke with the personnel at the court.
I do not remember who it was. I remember it was a female person. She said the magistrate was in
Provo from what I recall. She was not on island. I did not try the Chief Magistrate to see if he would
do it. I am not the investigating officer in this case. P.C. Higgs is the investigating officer. I was
part of the arresting team. I did not have an affidavit before arresting Mr. Simms. Mr. Simms was
not arrested at the scene of the supermarket. At the time Mr. Simms was arrested, he was not
engaging in conduct which amounted to physical violence. At the time Mr. Simms was arrested,
he was not attempting to engage in conduct which amounted to physical violence. He was running.
Mr. Simms was taken to the Grand Turk Police Station after his arrest. I cannot recall the date he
saw the magistrate…”
Hussien Higgs
11

31. The second and final Defence witness was Hussien Higgs. He is a Police Constable (No.
244) of the Royal Turks and Caicos Police Force, attached to the Operations and Response
Unit. He is the Investigating Officer in the matter.
32. In his Witness Statement, P.C. Higgs said that on Thursday 4th August 2022, he was on
duty at the Grand Turk Police Station along with Sergeant Emeial Shepherd, Police
Constable Marissa Brown and District Constable Lewis Bido. At around 6:30 p.m. he
received a telephone call via his cellular phone from one Mr. Rodney Dorsainvil, who was
