59. I think Mr Harris' interpretation that the ‘stay’ that is being considered in the Note is a stay
of taxation is incorrect. It is a stay of the judgment pending appeal, and the rule gives the
plaintiff the comfort of knowing that he has the spoils and costs of his litigation
immediately available if a losing defendant’s appeal is unsuccessful.
60. If the Note were with reference to a stay of taxation, it would be in conflict with Ord. 62
r.3(6)(b), which provides:
(b) where this order [for costs] is made at the conclusion of a cause or matter, the
party in whose favour it is made shall be entitled to have his costs taxed forthwith;
61. I also refer to Note 62/8/1, which provides inter alia:
“… a cause or matter … is concluded when the court in question has finally
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 18 of 20

determined the matters in issue, whether or not there is an appeal from that
determination”, per Saville J. Rafsanjan Pistachio Producers Corporation v.
Bank Leumi (U.K.) Ltd November 4, 1992, Review of Taxation (unrep.)
…
An order for the taxation of costs is not normally stayed pending an appeal
provided that repayment is secured (Minnesota Mining and Manufacturing
Company v. Rennicks (UK) Ltd (No 2) [1992] R.P.C. 331, Aldous J. (Patents
Court).
The court has power to order a stay of taxation on terms, e.g. that a sum of money
be paid pending appeal (subject to terms as to repayment and interest after a
successful appeal). The court considered that this might be the most appropriate
way of dealing with cases of complex and expensive taxation of costs (or indeed
complex and expensive enquiries as to damages). The Judge held that it required
special circumstances before a stay could be granted, but that the court’s
discretion could be made conditional upon any offer that might be made by the
paying party and any condition the court might impose as a term for granting a
stay (The Lubrizol Corporation v. Esso Petroleum Company Ltd, July 15, 1997,
Jacob J. (unrep.))
62. There is no authority before me that I can grant a stay of taxation simply on the
request/submission of counsel.
Conclusion
63. The general principle is that the successful party should have their costs of the litigation
unless there is some good reason to depart from that principle. Whilst I have given careful
consideration to the issues raised by Mr Harris, I am not in agreement that the elements of
the tort should be looked at as discrete or distinct issues. There was only one claim, and
the winner was the Defendant.
64. I am also not of the view that the other matters raised were discrete or distinct issues or
were raised unreasonably, and I do not consider, applying the principles described by the
authorities, that making an issue-based costs order would, in all the circumstances of this
case, it being an outright success by a defendant, be the correct result.
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 19 of 20

65. Also, I do not see that, for the reasons above, there are any grounds for ordering a stay of
taxation.
Disposition
66. The order is therefore that the Plaintiff shall pay the Defendant’s costs of the action, to be
taxed on the standard basis if not agreed.
67. I make no order on the issue of a stay of taxation such that the same may be reconsidered
by the taxing officer, if appropriate.
19th February 2026
The Hon. Justice Anthony S. Gruchot
Judge of the Supreme Court
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 20 of 20
court: Supreme Court of Turks and Caicos Islands
summary: A wholly successful defendant is ordinarily entitled to costs; issue-based apportionment and a stay of taxation were refused.

title: Majeur Delicieux v Kenroy Lawson (CL 52 of 2025) [2026] TCASC 12 (16 February 2026)
text:
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF THE ACTION NO. CL 52/25
TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
BETWEEN:
MAJEUR DELICIEUX Plaintiff
-and-
KENROY LAWSON Defendant
Before: The Hon. Mr Justice Anthony S. Gruchot
Appearances: Mr Keith James of James Law for the Plaintiff
Mr Ashwood Forbes of Forbes & Co for the Defendant
Hearing Date: 23rd September 2025
Venue: Supreme Court Annexe, Leeward Highway, Providenciales.
REASONS
1. On 26 September 2025, I dismissed a claim for enforcement of an order for possession of
residential premises granted by the Magistrate’s Court on 1 May 2025 and said I would
provide written reasons for the decision. These are those reasons.
Background
2. On 13 June 2025, the Plaintiff filed an Expedited Originating Summons seeking an order
for possession of residential premises together with an ‘eviction notice’. The application
was predicated on the order made by the learned Magistrate in the following terms:
CL 52/25 – Majeur Delicieux -v- Kenroy Lawson
Page 1 of 14

“IT IS ORDERED THAT:
1) The Defendant vacate the rented [a]partment on or before the 31st of May
2025.
2) The Defendant pay the Plaintiff the rent for the month of May 2025. The
rent to be paid even if the Defendant vacates the rented [a]partment
before 31st May 2025
3) There be no order as to costs”
3. The above mischaracterises ‘rent’. If the Plaintiff has lawfully terminated the Defendant’s
right to occupy the premises, then rent is no longer due and payable. What may be claimed
is mesne profits for wrongful occupation.
4. The application was supported by an affidavit sworn by the Plaintiff on 3 June 2025, in
which the following facts are averred:
a. On 27th October 2024, the Plaintiff entered into a verbal month-to-month tenancy
agreement with the Defendant for the rental of a two-bedroom apartment. The
agreed monthly rent was $2,000.00 per month.
b. The Plaintiff alleges that the Defendant refused to pay the monthly rent when it
became due on 27 February 2025, and the Plaintiff purported to forfeit the lease
by way of a letter1 requesting that the Defendant vacate the apartment.
c. Proceedings were commenced on 7th April 2025 in the Magistrate’s Court, Grand
Turk.
d. On 16 May 2025, the Defendant made a payment for the month of May but did
not vacate the premises.
5. On 27 June 2025, the Defendant swore an affidavit in response to this application. In that
affidavit, he avers:
a. That he had no issue with paying the rent, but there was a dispute as to when the
1 As the letter was not produced to this Court, it is assumed that it was a notice terminating the tenancy, and it
complied with section 45(3) Registered Land Act (Cap. 9.01) – See para. 43 below. Alternatively, it was treated as a
Notice Before Forfeiture as required by section 56 of the Registered Land Act (Cap. 9.01), specifying the relevant
breach of the tenancy, and giving the Defendant a reasonable time to remedy the breach.
CL 52/25 – Majeur Delicieux -v- Kenroy Lawson
Page 2 of 14

rent was due.
b. That he and his wife and three dependents occupy the premises, and they had
been unable to find suitable alternative accommodation, and requested more time.
6. The application came before me on 30 July 2025, at which time I raised certain issues with
Counsel and requested that they provide written submissions on those issues.
Issues raised by the Court
7. The civil jurisdiction of the Magistrate's Court is set out in Part XII of the Magistrate’s Court
Act (Cap. 2.03) (‘MCA’). Section 108(1)(i) MCA provides:
108. The Magistrate shall have jurisdiction within the Islands to hear and
render judgment––
…
(i) in all suits for specific performance or for the reforming, delivering up
or cancelling of any agreement of the sale, purchase or lease of any
property where in the case of a sale or purchase, the purchase money, or
in the case of a loan, the value of the property, shall not exceed $25,000;
(Emphasis added)
8. The above is the only reference in the MCA to a lease of property. I refer to this provision,
as it appears that it is a common understanding that it is the provision that gives the
Magistrate’s Court jurisdiction to hear possession claims.
9. Section 157 of the Registered Land Act (Cap. 9.01) (‘RLA’) provides:
Jurisdiction of Courts
157. Civil suits and proceedings relating to the ownership or the possession
of land, or to a lease or charge, registered under this [Act], or to any interest
in any such land, lease or charge, being an interest which is registered or
registrable under this [Act], or being an interest which is referred to in section
28 of this [Act] shall (notwithstanding the provisions of any other [Act]), be
tried by the Supreme Court, or where the value of the subject matter in dispute
does not exceed $10,000, by the Magistrate’s Court.
CL 52/25 – Majeur Delicieux -v- Kenroy Lawson
Page 3 of 14

10. Section 3 RLA provides:
Reconciliation with other laws
3. Except as otherwise provided in this [Act], no other written law and no
practice or procedure relating to land shall apply to land registered
under this [Act] so far as it is inconsistent with this [Act]:
(Emphasis added)
11. In Mums Incorporated and Tham-Hong Tan -v- Cayman Capital Trust Company,
B.V. Randall and E.G. Randall,2 the Court of Appeal, in dealing with section 3 of the
Registered Land Law, Georges JA delivering the judgment of the Court, said:
“While concepts of English land law both before and after 1925 may provide a
useful backdrop against which to view the RLL,3 they should not be permitted to
intrude upon its interpretation.”
12. Section 3 reads:
“Except as otherwise provided in this Law, no other Law and no practice or
procedure relating to land should apply to land registered under this Law so far
as it is inconsistent with this law.”
13. The Court held:
“It would appear from these provisions that the RLL is intended to cover
completely the matters pertaining to the registration of land and dealings in
registered land with which it purports to deal.” (My emphasis)
14. Section 3 of the RLA is in identical terms4 as to section 3 of the RLL. The RLA is intended
to cover completely matters pertaining to the registration of land and dealings with
registered land.
15. The questions on which I sought submissions were as follows:
a. What jurisdiction did the Magistrate have to issue the order for possession?
2 [2000] CILR 131 (March 28th 1990).
3 The Registered Land Law (Cayman Islands).
4 Save for the word ‘Law’ which is replaced with ‘Ordinance’ (now ‘Act’).
CL 52/25 – Majeur Delicieux -v- Kenroy Lawson
Page 4 of 14

b. What jurisdiction does the Supreme Court have to enforce an order of the
Magistrate?
c. What view do the parties take as to the observation that 'loan' in section 108(1)(i)
MCA is possibly a typographical error and should be read as 'lease'?
d. What do the words 'the value of the subject matter in dispute' mean in section 157
of the RLA?
Discussion
16. In my view, section 108(i) of the MCA does not confer a jurisdiction to make
possession/eviction orders, but rather confers a jurisdiction to hear claims inter alia for
specific performance or for the reforming, delivering up or cancelling of any lease. If I am
wrong on that issue, and section 108(i) does grant jurisdiction to grant possession, by
operation of section 3 RLA,5 the provisions of the RLA would prevail.
17. It appears to me that the word ‘loan’ in section 108(i) of the MCA is an error and should, in
fact, be the word ‘lease’. This would reflect a legislative intention to impose a jurisdictional
limit for claims which may be heard by the Magistrate’s Court for specific performance or
for the reforming, delivering up or cancelling of any lease involving property valued at less
than $25,000.00. I am fortified in that suggestion as not only did Mr James agree, but section
67 of the County Courts Act, 1888 (England and Wales)6 provided:
"The court shall have and exercise all the powers and authority of the High Court
in the actions and matters hereinafter mentioned; (that is to say)
…
(4) For specific performance of or for the reforming, delivering up, or cancelling
of any agreement for the sale, purchase, or lease of any property, where in the
case of a sale or purchase the purchase money, or in the case of a lease the value
of the property, shall not exceed the sum of five hundred pounds:" (Emphasis
5 See para. 9 above.
6 This provision has gone through several replacement Acts, the most recent being the County Courts Act 1984 in
which section 23(d) provides ‘proceedings for the specific performance, or for the rectification, delivery up or
cancellation, of any agreement for the sale, purchase or lease of any property, where, in the case of a sale or
purchase, the purchase money, or in the case of a lease, the value of the property, does not exceed the county
court limit.’ (Emphasis added)
CL 52/25 – Majeur Delicieux -v- Kenroy Lawson
Page 5 of 14

added)
18. Section 157 RLA does grant a limited jurisdiction to the Magistrate’s Court to grant
possession orders with respect to leases which are registered or registerable under the RLA.
Section 46 of the RLA deals with the registration of leases and provides:
Registration of leases
46. A lease for a specified period exceeding two years, or for the life of the
lessor or of the lessee, or a lease which contains an option whereby the lessee
may require the lessor to grant him a further term or terms which, together
with the original term, exceed two years, shall be in the prescribed form, and
shall be completed by––
(a) opening a register in respect of the lease in the name of the lessee;
(b) filing the lease; and
(c) noting the lease in the incumbrances section of the register of the
lessor’s land or lease
19. In this matter, the lease is not registered as it was a verbal periodic tenancy. It was not
capable of being registered as, plainly:
a. it is not possible to file a verbal agreement; and
b. it does not meet the requisite requirements for registration.
20. In saying the above, I acknowledge that section 46 deals with the ‘completion’ of a lease
exceeding two years, etc. however, in my view, a purposive interpretation of the section
must mean that a lease that does not comply with those requirements is not capable of
registration, otherwise the words in section 157 “being an interest which is registered or
registrable under this [Act],” would be otiose.
21. However, section 157 also grants a jurisdiction to the Magistrate’s Court in respect of any
interest which is referred to in section 28 RLA. Section 28(d) provides:
Overriding interests
28. Unless the contrary is expressed in the register, all registered land shall be
CL 52/25 – Majeur Delicieux -v- Kenroy Lawson
Page 6 of 14

subject to such of the following overriding interests as may for the time being
subsist and affect the same, without their being noted on the register––
…
(d) leases or agreements for leases for a term not exceeding two years,
and periodic tenancies within the meaning of section 2 of this [Act];
22. A periodic tenancy is defined as being:
“periodic tenancy” means a tenancy from year to year, half year to half year,
quarter to quarter, month to month, week to week or the like;
23. Prima facie, then, the learned Magistrate did have jurisdiction (subject to the provisions of
the RLA)7 to grant the order; however, that jurisdiction is subject to the proviso that “the
value of the subject matter does not exceed $10,000.00.” (Emphasis added)
24. What then does ‘subject matter’ mean?
25. In Angel -v- Jay8 the Court considered the scope of the jurisdiction of the County Court
under section 67(4) of the County Courts Act 1888 (England and Wales).9 This was an
appeal to the King’s Bench Division (Darling and Bucknill JJ) from the decision of the
Brompton County Court who considered that it had jurisdiction. The matter concerned a
sub-lease for a period of three years at an annual rent of £45.00. The value of the freehold
exceeded £500.00, but the value of the interest demised to the plaintiff was less and hence
likewise the demise to the defendant.
26. Darling J, in concluding that the County Court had no jurisdiction said:
“Now, it is admitted that the value of the property, if by property is meant the thing
leased - that is, the house and garden - was more than 500 pounds but the value
of the interest which the lessor had was less than 500 pounds and therefore the
value of the interest which he granted to the underlessee was also necessarily less
than that amount. Counsel for the plaintiff contends that we ought to have regard
to the value of the interest granted by the lease, and not to the value of the property,
7 See footnote 1 above.
8 [1908-10] All ER Rep 470; [1911] 1 KB 666.
9 See para. 17 above.
CL 52/25 – Majeur Delicieux -v- Kenroy Lawson
Page 7 of 14

the occupation of which was granted by the lease. No doubt if you look at the case
of a sale under the section it is provided that the purchase money is to be taken as
the measure in determining whether the value of the property exceeds 500 pounds.
Whether that was deliberately or accidentally done in that case, I come to the
conclusion that this standard has not been applied to the case of a lease. In the
case of a lease nothing is said about the rent to be paid or premiums, and nothing
is said as to how you are to ascertain the value of the lease. We can only look at
the plain words used in the statute, as WILLS, J, said in R v Judge Whitehorne (3).
As the statute says in the case of a lease that regard must be had to the value of
the property, we must hold that what we must look at is the value of the property
demised, and not the consideration given for the interest demised. The county
court judge had, therefore, no jurisdiction to give the equitable remedy which he
gave in this case, and the result is that this appeal must be allowed with costs.
(Emphasis added)
27. The wording of section 157 RLA is slightly different in its limiting jurisdiction, as it refers
to “the value of the subject matter in dispute” rather than “the value of the property”.
28. In National Housing Corporation -v- Okau10 the Court of Appeal considered whether the
Magistrate’s Court had jurisdiction to make an eviction order where the value of the
property in dispute exceeded the limit provided for in the Magistrates' Court (Civil
Jurisdiction) Act [CAP 130] which provided at section 1:
1. Jurisdiction of the Magistrates Court in civil matters
The Magistrates’ Court … shall have jurisdiction to try all civil proceedings
–
(a) In which the amount claimed or the value of the subject matter does
not exceed VT1,000,000 except claims relating to permanent physical
damage to a person:
(b) Relating to disputes between landlords and their tenants where there
is no claim for damages or compensation or if there is such a claim if
10 [2013] VUCA 21; Civil Appeal Case 16 of 2013 (26 July 2013), (Republic of Vanuatu)
CL 52/25 – Majeur Delicieux -v- Kenroy Lawson
Page 8 of 14

the value does not exceed VT2,000,000. (Emphasis added)
29. The issue in the claim before the Magistrate was a “straightforward claim for an eviction
order”;11 however, on the facts, the court did not consider that the application was a landlord
and tenant matter, as it was a claim for possession/eviction for failure to perform an
agreement to acquire the head leasehold interest held by the plaintiff, and so considered
what was meant by ‘the value of the subject matter’.
30. The judgment was given for the court.12 Per Vincent Lunabek CJ:
“The question therefore is whether the Magistrates’ Court had jurisdiction under
s. 1(a) as a civil proceeding in which the amount claimed or the value of the subject
matter does not exceed VT 1,000,000.
The claim for an order for eviction was not a money claim. What was claimed was
possession of the leasehold land of which NHC was the registered lessee. Did the
subject matter of the claim exceed VT1,000,000?
The Agreement provided for a purchase price of VT1,138,000, but that was back
in 2006. There was evidence before the court of the current value of the leasehold
title in the form of a valuation certificate from Tahi Consultant and Real Estate
Services dated 7 October 2011 which is as the value at that date to be
VT1,500,000.”
31. The court went on to cite Angel and continued:
“In this case what was an issue was the leasehold title of the property, not a
freehold title. It is not possible in the case of a leasehold title to argue that the
value is something less than the value of the leasehold because a lessee has no
residual interest in that title that can be valued separately from the leasehold
interest.
In our opinion in a case where in order for possession or eviction is sought by the
lessor the value of the subject matter before the court is the value of the leasehold
interest. In this case that value was clearly above VT1,000,000. The Magistrates’
11 At para. 14.
12 It was a convened Panel of seven judges.
CL 52/25 – Majeur Delicieux -v- Kenroy Lawson
Page 9 of 14

Court therefore did not have jurisdiction.”
32. The jurisdictional provision in Angel was similar to that in section 103(i) MCA, but the
relief being sought was the cancellation of an agreement to rent, not an order for
possession/eviction following termination of a tenancy, hence the court held it was the value
of the property and not the value of the leasehold/sub-leasehold interest that should be
considered.
33. In this matter, the jurisdiction arises under the RLA, and for the purposes of section 157
RLA, I am of the view that the ‘subject matter’ is the leasehold interest.
34. Mr James submits that when the matter was before the learned Magistrate, value was not
put into issue. He submits:
“…that even if the two-bedroom apartment (the subject matter) exceeds
$25,000.00, the defence at the time raised no issue as to value or challenged the
Magistrates’ jurisdiction pursuant to section 132 (i) of the Magistrate’s Court
[Act].13 The Defendant did not dispute that the Plaintiff was entitled to possession
of the [a]partment. In fact, the Magistrate[‘s] order was based on the
Defendant[‘s] own timeline [which] his Counsel submitted for him to vacate the
apartment (31st of May 2025). Since no challenge existed at the time as to the value
of the subject matter or the Plaintiff’s right to possession of the apartment. We
submit that the Honourable Magistrate cannot be said to have exceeded her
jurisdiction.”
35. The above is not, in my view, correct, and Mr James has imported into section 103(i) MCA,
the definition of ‘subject matter’ from section 157 RLA. And as I have said, I am not of the
view that section 103(i) gives jurisdiction to make possession or eviction orders.
36. He relies on a case from the Supreme Court of the Republic of Vanuatu, Peter Masel
Nikiatu and others -v- (1) Keil Wilson and Jeffrey Moses (First Defendants) and
Republic of Vanuatu (Second Defendant)14 in which it was asserted that the Magistrate’s
Court had jurisdiction to grant eviction orders, but only where the title of the claimant lessee
13 Mr James appears to be referring to the MCA as at the 2014 Revised Laws. There have been amendments to
that, which have had the consequence of renumbering the sections.
14 Case No. 15/814 SC/Civil.
CL 52/25 – Majeur Delicieux -v- Kenroy Lawson
Page 10 of 14

is not in dispute and the value of the leasehold is less than VT1,000,000.15 Nikiatu relied
on Okau, submitting that the Magistrate had no jurisdiction as the value of the leasehold
was over VT1,000,000; however, the court went on to hold that as there was no valuation
evidence before it, it could not be said that the Magistrate’s court had exceeded its
jurisdiction. I do not agree with that proposition. To my mind, the Magistrate’s Court either
has jurisdiction, or it does not. I do not think it is right to say, as the learned judge appears
to have done in Nikiatu, and as Mr James submits, that if the valuation of the subject matter
has not been put into issue, the Magistrate’s Court cannot be said to have exceeded its
jurisdiction.
37. Mr Forbes submits:
“The Learned Magistrate erred in law when she failed to considered (sic) the
salient issue of the case prior to making the eviction Order, which was the value
of the subject matter, which was overlooked by the Bench and Counsel during the
trial.”
38. There is, I think, an obligation for the Magistrate to consider whether it has jurisdiction to
make the orders it intends to make. In circumstances such as in the present case, this would
mean that the Magistrate should carry out an exercise to ascertain, as far as possible, the
value of the subject matter. I am fortified in that view, as in Okau, reference was made to
section 4 of the Magistrate’s Court (Civil Jurisdiction) Act which provides:
Decisions on value
(1) Where the value of property or a claim cannot be precisely given a
plaintiff may give an estimated value in his claim.
(2) When an estimated value is given in accordance with subsection (1) the
court shall try the question of value as a preliminary issue.
(3) When the court has heard the evidence and representations on value
under Section (2) it shall determine whether or not the claim comes
15 This is derived from section 1 of the Magistrate’s Court (Civil Jurisdiction) Act [Cap. 130] which provides: “The
Magistrate’s court shall have jurisdiction to try all civil proceedings – (a) in which the amount claimed or the value
of the subject matter does not exceed VT1,000,000…” (Emphasis added)
CL 52/25 – Majeur Delicieux -v- Kenroy Lawson
Page 11 of 14

within its jurisdiction and if it decides that it does not, shall… refer the
claim to the Supreme Court.
39. Whilst I am unaware of any similar provision within the Turks and Caicos Islands
legislation, it does not detract from the principle that the Court should satisfy itself it has
jurisdiction to do what it intends to do, a fortiori when such jurisdiction is qualified.
40. What then is the value of the subject matter? I was not addressed on that issue by Counsel.
41. The only evidence before me as to the value of the subject matter was in the affidavit of the
Plaintiff as set out at paragraph 4.a above. That evidence is unchallenged.
42. This is a month-to-month periodic tenancy, with an agreed rent of $2,000.00 per month.
Whilst the payment date is in dispute, the amount of rent is not.
43. Section 45 RLA provides:
Periodic tenancies
45. (1) Where in any lease the term is not specified and no provision is made
for the giving of notice to terminate the tenancy, the lease shall be deemed
to have created a periodic tenancy.
(2) Where the proprietor of land permits the exclusive occupation of the
land or any part thereof by any other person at a rent but without any
agreement in writing, that occupation shall be deemed to constitute a
periodic tenancy.
(3) The period of a periodic tenancy created by this subsection shall be
the period by reference to which the rent is payable, and the tenancy may
be determined by either party giving to the other notice, the length of
which shall, subject to any other written law, be not less than the period
of the tenancy and shall expire on one of the days on which rent is payable.
44. Where a tenancy is for a fixed term, then following the reasoning in Okau, I would posit
the view that that the value of the lease is the rent payable under the residuary of the term,
notwithstanding that would create an unsatisfactory situation where the Magistrate’s Court
would in one month have no jurisdiction to make a possession/eviction order, but could
CL 52/25 – Majeur Delicieux -v- Kenroy Lawson
Page 12 of 14

have jurisdiction in a following month.
45. Periodic tenancies have little or no future value, particularly monthly or weekly periodic
tenancies, which, as set out in paragraph 43 above, can be terminated by giving notice of
just 1 rental period, in this case $2,000.00.
46. If I am correct in my assessment of how to approach the valuation of the subject matter in a
claim for possession/eviction, then the learned Magistrate would have had jurisdiction to
make an order for possession, assuming it had been lawfully terminated.
47. The question that then arises is what jurisdiction does the Supreme Court have to enforce
an order of the Magistrate’s Court?
48. There are no provisions in the MCA with respect to the enforcement of possession/eviction
orders. Money judgments can be enforced by the issuance of a writ of execution to levy
against goods,16 and where insufficient goods cannot be found, the matter can be referred to
the Supreme Court to execute against the land and tenements of the judgment debtor, as if
the judgment had been given in the Supreme Court.17 Additionally, the judgment creditor
can apply for a judgment summons calling upon the judgment debtor to show cause why he
should not be committed to prison.
49. There is also no provision for an order for possession/eviction to be transferred to the
Supreme Court for enforcement18 by way of issue of a writ of possession under Ord. 45
r.3.19
50. Mr James, to his credit, as this was his application, submits:
“On the issue of the Supreme Court’s jurisdiction to enforce an order from the
Magistrate’s Court we submit that where there is no legislation, rules or practice
to grant jurisdiction to the Supreme Court to enforce a judgment from the
Magistrates Court, the Supreme as a creature of statute likely lacks jurisdiction.”
16 Section 119 MCA.
17 Section 120 MCA.
18 Compared with, for example, section 93(5) Employment Act (Cap. 17.08) which provides – (5) Orders, decisions
and awards of the Labour Tribunal shall be enforceable in the Supreme Court as though they were orders or
judgements of that court.
19 Civil Procedure Rules 2000.
CL 52/25 – Majeur Delicieux -v- Kenroy Lawson
Page 13 of 14

51. I agree. The application before me was incorrectly commenced by way of an Expedited
Originating Summons; however, even if the Plaintiff had sought leave to issue a writ of
possession based on the learned Magistrate’s order, the Supreme Court is bereft of any
jurisdiction which would allow for such a grant of leave. Accordingly, I dismissed the
application.
16th February 2026
The Hon. Justice Anthony S. Gruchot
Judge of the Supreme Court
CL 52/25 – Majeur Delicieux -v- Kenroy Lawson
Page 14 of 14
court: Supreme Court of Turks and Caicos Islands
summary: Supreme Court dismissed enforcement application: Magistrate likely had jurisdiction, but Supreme Court lacks statutory power to enforce eviction orders.

title: R v Kervens Cherenfant (sentencing) (CR 19 of 2025) [2026] TCASC 13 (12 February 2026)
text:
IN THE SUPREME COURT
TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
CRIMINAL DIVISION
CR 19/2025
BETWEEN:
REX
Vs.
KERVENS CHERENFANT
______________________________________
SENTENCING REASONS
_____________________________________
Before: The Hon. Mr. Justice Chris Selochan
Appearances:
Crown: Ms. Tamika Grant
Defence: Mr. Ashwood Forbes
Hearing Dates: 28th January, 2026, 6th February 2026 and 12th February
2026.
Venue: Annex, Supreme Court, Providenciales
Handed Down: 12th February 2026.
BACKGROUND
1. The Defendant, Kervens Cherenfant, has pleaded guilty to the following two counts
on an indictment:
COUNT 1
1

STATEMENT OF OFFENCE
SEXUAL ACTIVITY WITH A CHILD: Contrary to section 11(1) of the Sexual Offences
Act of the laws of the Turks and Caicos Islands.
PARTICULARS OF OFFENCE
That you, sometime in January 2025, in Grand Turk, Turks and Caicos Islands, being a person
aged 18 or over, intentionally sexually touched X, a child under the age of 16 years old, by
penetrating her vagina with a part of your body, namely your penis.
COUNT 2
STATEMENT OF OFFENCE
SEXUAL ACTIVITY WITH A CHILD: Contrary to section 11(1) of the Sexual Offences
Act of the laws of the Turks and Caicos Islands.
PARTICULARS OF OFFENCE
That you, sometime on Wednesday February 5th, 2025, in Grand Turk, Turks and Caicos
Islands, being a person aged 18 or over, intentionally sexually touched X, a child under the age
of 16 years old, by penetrating her vagina with a part of your body, namely your penis.
2. Given the nature of the offences, I have replaced the name of the Virtual Complainant
with “X” in order to ensure that her identity remains confidential.
3. At the time of the incident, the Defendant was 18 years of age and X was 13 years of
age.
4. The following is a summary of the Agreed Facts as between the Crown and the
Defence which formed the basis of sentencing:
2

a. X was born in August 2011 and was thirteen (13) years of age at the time of the
incidents. She attends high school and lives with her mother.
b. The Accused was born on 30th August 2006 and was eighteen (18) years old at the
time of the incidents.
c. X met the Defendant at a grocery one day. He offered her a ride and asked for her
number and thereafter contacted her regularly via text messages. He spoke with
her via Snapchat (an app where the messages automatically delete themselves after
they are read and notifies the sender if the messages are screenshot or saved in the
app) on her brother’s Ipad. X considered the Defendant to be her boyfriend. The
conversations were initially friendly and casual but gradually became
inappropriate and the Defendant began calling her “honey” and “babe”. He also
started sending her messages of a sexual nature, including whether they could
engage in sexual intercourse and requesting that she participate in sexual acts,
despite knowing that she was a virgin. On the occasions when he came to visit her,
he would signal his presence by flashing the lights on his vehicle outside her home.
d. The intercourse between the two occurred on or about 15th January 2025 and on 5th
February 2025. On both occasions this happened in his car parked in the bushes
near a barbershop close to her home. The Defendant asked her to keep their
intercourse secret.
e. On 5th February 2025, X’s mother saw her daughter get inside a white car outside
of her home. She got dressed and went outside where she saw the car rocking and
she knocked on the window. The car then sped away. X later returned home and
her mom confronted her, but she did not tell her mom what happened until the
following day. X told her mother that she was with a man called Kervens, that they
had sex two times and that he is twenty years old. As a result of this, her mother
took her to the police station where a report was made.
f. An investigation commenced and the Defendant was arrested at the Howard
Hamilton International Airport on a one-way ticket to Haiti to renew his passport.
He was taken to the Chalk Sound Police Station and interviewed where he gave a
“No Comment” interview.
g. The Defendant later made an admission to the officer at the station that he had
engaged in sexual intercourse with X on two occasions, following which a
statement was recorded from him under caution.
3

h. A second interview was then done with the Defendant and he admitted to having
sexual intercourse with X on 5th February 2025 and, prior to that, sometime in
January 2025.
i. Since making the report to the police, X experiences feelings of disgust and self-
blame for having given the Defendant her phone number. She has also experienced
fear and anxiety which she is working on during therapy. She also expressed fear
that if released from custody, the Defendant might harm her or confront her for
reporting him and that she felt that he knew what he was doing was wrong
because he asked her to keep it a secret.
j. X’s sense of safety and emotional wellbeing was significantly affected. She
reported feeling nervous about the possibility of seeing the Defendant again and
experiencing distress when reflecting on the events. She also stated that, at the time
the report was made, her academic performance was negatively impacted and she
felt uncomfortable attending school. These feelings have improved since therapy.
SUBMISSIONS
5. In his plea in mitigation and Submissions on sentencing on behalf of the Defendant,
Mr. Ashwood Forbes submitted as follows:
a. The Defendant is a Haitian national who has been living and working in Grand Turk
since 2023.
b. He was 18 years of the age at the time of the offence.
c. He is extremely remorseful for his actions.
d. He was of the impression that the X was 17 years of age.
e. The Defendant previously worked at Grand Turk Liquors.
f. He is a person of good character.
g. The UK’s Sentencing Guidelines can be utilised in which case the Defendant would
fall within Category 1A in respect of this offence.
h. There was no trial so the Defendant did not have to relive the incident.
i. The aggravating factors are outweighed by the mitigating factors.
4

j. The starting point should be reduced to two years, in which case the court can
consider imposing a suspended sentence.
6. Ms. Tamika Grant on behalf of the Crown submitted the following:
a. The Crown acknowledges that the Defendant has always indicated an intention to
plead guilty and did so at the earliest opportunity.
b. The Defendant has no previous convictions, is young and appears to be remorseful.
c. There is a large five year age gap between the Defendant and X.
d. There was a significant degree of planning because the Defendant pursued X for a
number of months.
e. There was grooming involved since the conversation became increasingly sexualised
and this was her first time engaging in sexual intercourse.
f. The incidents have caused psychological distress to X. In this regard, reference was
made to the Pre-Sentencing Report and the Victim Impact Statement.
g. The offence falls within Category 1A of the UK Sentencing Guidelines. The starting
point is five years and this should be uplifted because of the aggravating factors.
h. Although there are mitigating factors, they are outweighed by the aggravating factors.
i. The final sentence should not approach two years so a suspended sentence would not
be considered.
ANALYSIS OF THE COURT
7. Section 11 of the Sexual Offences Act deal with the offence of sexual activity with a
child and provides as follows:
11. (1) A person aged eighteen or over (A) commits an offence if—
(a) he intentionally touches another person (B);
(b) the touching is sexual; and (c) either—
5

(i) B is under sixteen and A does not reasonably believe that B is sixteen or over; or
(ii) B is under thirteen.
(2) A person guilty of an offence under this section, if the touching involved—
(a) penetration of B’s anus or vagina with a part of A’s body or anything else;
(b) penetration of B’s mouth with A’s penis;
(c) penetration of A’s anus or vagina with a part of B’s body; or (d) penetration of A’s mouth with
B’s penis,
is liable, on conviction on indictment, to imprisonment for a term not exceeding fourteen years.
(3) Unless subsection (2) applies, a person guilty of an offence under this section is liable—
(a) on summary conviction, to imprisonment for a term not exceeding six months, or to a fine of
$10,000, or to both;
(b) on conviction on indictment, to imprisonment for a term not exceeding fourteen years.
8. In Benjamin v R1 Wooding CJ outlined the following five principal objects of
sentencing:
i. The retributive or denunciatory, which is the same as the punitive;
ii. the deterrent, vis-à-vis potential offenders;
iii. the deterrent vis-vis the particular offender then being sentenced;
iv. the preventative, which aims at preventing the particular offender from again
offending by incarcerating him for a long period; and
v. the rehabilitative, which contemplates the rehabilitation of the particular offender
so that he might resume his place as a law-abiding member of society.
9. In this matter I consider the retributive or denunciatory object and objects of
deterrence vis-à-vis potential offenders, deterrence vis-à-vis the particular offender as
well as the rehabilitative principle, to be predominant.
10. This analysis will relate to each count on the indictment.
1 (1964) 7 WIR 459 at pages 460-461
6

11. Both parties have submitted that, in the absence of Sentencing Guidelines in the TCI
for the offence under section 11 of the Sexual Offences Act, the UK’s Sentencing
Council’s Sentencing Guidelines for Use in the Crown Court (“the UK’s Guidelines”) in
respect of an offence under section 9 of the UK’s Sexual Offences Act 2003 can be
utilised.
12. Section 9 of the UK’s Sexual Offences Act 2003 provides:
Sexual activity with a child
(1) A person aged 18 or over (A) commits an offence if—
(a) he intentionally touches another person (B),
(b) the touching is sexual, and
(c) either—
(i) B is under 16 and A does not reasonably believe that B is 16 or over, or
(ii) B is under 13.
(2) A person guilty of an offence under this section, if the touching involved—
(a) penetration of B’s anus or vagina with a part of A’s body or anything else,
(b) penetration of B’s mouth with A’s penis,
(c) penetration of A’s anus or vagina with a part of B’s body, or
(d) penetration of A’s mouth with B’s penis,
is liable, on conviction on indictment, to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 14 years.
(3) Unless subsection (2) applies, a person guilty of an offence under this section is liable—
(a) on summary conviction, to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 6 months or to a fine
not exceeding the statutory maximum or both;
(b) on conviction on indictment, to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 14 years.
13. Section 11 of the TCI’s Sexual Offences Act is essentially on the same terms as section
9 of the UK’s Sexual Offences Act, with both carrying a maximum penalty on
indictment of a term of imprisonment not exceeding 14 years. I am therefore in
agreement with counsel that the UK Guidelines can be properly used in sentencing for
these offences.
7

14. The first step in sentencing according to the UK Guidelines is to ascribe a category to
the offence. In order to determine the category of the offence, the Court is required to
assess Harm and Culpability.
15. In respect of Harm, an offence falls within Category 1 if it involves penetration of the
vagina or anus (using body or object) or penile penetration of the mouth, in either case
by or of the victim.
16. As such, since the offences in the indictment to which the Defendant has pleaded
guilty involved sexual intercourse (penetration of the vagina) by the Defendant, it is a
Category 1 offence.
17. In respect of Culpability, the UK Guidelines list the following as factors which go to
show that an offence falls within Category A:
a. Use of alcohol/drugs on the victim to facilitate the offence.
b. Significant degree of planning.
c. Offender acts together with others to commit the offence.
d. Grooming behaviour used against the victim.
e. Abuse of trust.
f. Use of threats (including blackmail).
g. Sexual images of the victim recorded, retained, solicited or shared.
h. Specific targeting of a particularly vulnerable child.
i. Offender lied about age.
j. Significant disparity in age.
k. Commercial exploitation and/or motivation.
l. Offence racially or religiously motivated.
m. Offence motivated by, or demonstrating, hostility to the victim based on his or her
sexual orientation (or presumed sexual orientation) or transgender identity (or
presumed transgender identity).
n. Offence motivated by, or demonstrating hostility to the victim based on his or her
disability (or presumed disability).
18. I consider the offences on the indictment to be Culpability A for the following reasons:
8

a. There was a significant degree of planning as well as some degree of grooming
behaviour. In this regard, I note the following from the Agreed Facts:
The conversations were initially friendly and casual but gradually became inappropriate
and the Defendant began calling her “honey” and “babe”. He also started sending her
messages of a sexual nature, including whether they could engage in sexual intercourse
and requesting that she participate in sexual acts, despite knowing she was a virgin. On
the occasions when he came to visit her, he would signal his presence by flashing the lights
on his vehicle outside her home.
b. There was a clear abuse of trust, particularly since the Defendant knew that X was
attending school and was a virgin.
c. There was a relatively significant disparity in age. The Defendant was 18 and the
victim was 13 at the time of the incidents.
19. The Offence therefore falls as a Category 1A offence.
20. According to the UK Guidelines, the starting point for a Category 1A offence is five
years custody. The Category Range is four to ten years custody.
21. I pause to note at this point that there appear to be no reported cases involving
sentencing for this offence in the Turks and Caicos Islands. One of the reasons for this
is probably because the Sexual Offences Act is a relatively recent 2020 piece of
legislation.
22. The UK Guidelines provide that the starting point of five years can be adjusted
upwards if there are multiple factors listed in one category. I have noted three factors
in paragraph 18 above.
23. I have considered the Pre-sentencing Report which was competently prepared by
Kerren Griffith (Probation & Parole Officer) and Letitia Wiltshire (Probation Manager)
of the Department of Rehabilitation and Community Supervision, as well as the victim
impact statement.
9

24. I note that the Pre-sentencing Report and the victim impact statement show that the
Virtual Complainant has experienced feelings of disgust and self-blame for having
given the Defendant her phone number, as well as fear and anxiety.
25. Paragraph 6 of page 6 of the Pre-sentencing Report states, inter alia:
“..The impact on the victim and her mother, as reported, has been significant. The victim has
experienced emotional distress requiring therapeutic intervention...”
26. In addition, her sense of safety and emotional wellbeing was significantly affected, and
her academic performance was also affected. These are confirmed by paragraphs 7 and
8 of the Agreed Facts. I note also that, as part of the Agreed Facts, X felt that the
Defendant knew what he was doing was wrong because he asked her to keep it a
secret.
27. As a result, I will adjust the starting point upwards from five years to eight years.
28. I then move to mitigating factors, the presence of which according to the UK
Guidelines will result in a downward adjustment to the Adjusted Starting Point. I take
into account the following mitigating factors:
a. The Defendant was only 18 years of age at the time of the incident and therefore
lacked maturity.
b. The Defendant is of good character and has no previous convictions.
c. The Defendant has expressed some degree of remorse for his actions. I glean this
from his attorney’s plea in mitigation as well as from Paragraph 5, page 6 of the
Pre-Sentencing Report, which provides:
With regards to the offense, Mr. Cherenfant accepted some responsibility by entering a
guilty plea. However, elements of minimization and limited insight remain. He maintains
that he did not know the victim’s true age at the time of the incidents, despite
acknowledging that he was aware she was still in school. He expressed that he now
recognizes he should have confirmed her age and intends to exercise caution in future
10

relationships. Although he does not view himself as having committed a crime, he stated
that he pled guilty because he should have known better.
d. The level of recidivism is Medium according to the Pre-Sentencing Report. The
Report further states at paragraph 7 of page 6:
The Static-99R actuarial tool was utilized to evaluate Mr. Cherenfant. He is in the average
risk category, which suggests that he shares traits with a large number of adult male sexual
offenders. His level of risk should be considered in conjunction with his minimization, his
developmental factors, and the case's overall context.
29. In these circumstances, taking into account these mitigating factors, I reduce the
adjusted starting point for each offence from eight years to six years.
30. It was agreed that the Defendant pleaded guilty at the earliest possible opportunity.
He is therefore entitled to a one third discount on his sentence of six years for each
offence.
31. The sentence on each count will therefore be a term of imprisonment of four years.
From this, time spent in custody on remand from 24th February 2025 will be taken into
account by way of an arithmetical deduction in his sentence2.
32. The sentences on both counts will run concurrently.
33. I will not be exercising my discretion to issue a Deportation Order.
34. The Defendant is a very young man and, taking into account time spent in custody,
will still be in his early twenties when he is released from prison.
35. It is hoped that he will return to the community and make a meaningful contribution,
and not repeat acts of this nature. According to the Pre-Sentencing Report, the
Defendant only made it to Form 3 in high school but has expressed an interest in
returning to school.
2 See Romeo Da Costa Hall v The Queen [2011] CCJ 6 (AJ) per the Honourable Mr. Justice Rolston Nelson at
paragraph 20
11

36. In the circumstances, and in order to achieve one of the key and most progressive
objectives of sentencing, being rehabilitation, I propose to order the Department of
Rehabilitation and Community Services to arrange with the Department of
Behavioural Health Services to conduct an assessment of the Defendant, including but
not limited to the risk of sexual recidivism, within ninety (90) days of the date of the
Order, and to offer any appropriate remedial and rehabilitative assistance to the
Defendant during his period of imprisonment arising out of this assessment.
37. In addition, I will order the Department of Rehabilitation and Community Services to
work with the Department of Behavioural Health Services to provide the Defendant
with any necessary behavioral therapy, counselling as well as vocational/ educational
support while he is in custody in order to strengthen his employability and prosocial
support upon release and to facilitate his successful reintegration into society.
THE DEFENDANT IS HEREBY SENTENCED AS FOLLOWS:
1. ON COUNT 1: SEXUAL ACTIVITY WITH A CHILD: Contrary to section 11(1) of the
Sexual Offences Act of the laws of the Turks and Caicos Islands:
The Defendant is sentenced to serve a term of imprisonment of four (4) years with the
time spent in custody from 24th February, 2025 to be taken into account.
2. ON COUNT 2: SEXUAL ACTIVITY WITH A CHILD: Contrary to section 11(1) of the
Sexual Offences Act of the laws of the Turks and Caicos Islands:
The Defendant is sentenced to serve a term of imprisonment of four (4) years with the
time spent in custody from 24th February 2025 to be taken into account.
3. Both sentences are to run concurrently.
4. The Department of Rehabilitation and Community Supervision is hereby ordered to
arrange with the Department of Behavioural Health Services to conduct an assessment
of the Defendant, including but not limited to the risk of sexual recidivism, within
ninety (90) days of the date of this Order and to offer any appropriate remedial and
12

rehabilitative assistance to the Defendant during his period of imprisonment arising
out of this assessment.
5. The Department of Rehabilitation and Community Supervision is also hereby ordered
to work with the Department of Behavioural Health Services to provide the Defendant
with any necessary behavioral therapy, counselling as well as vocational/ educational
support while he is in custody in order to strengthen his employability and prosocial
support upon release and facilitate his successful reintegration into society.
Dated 12th February, 2026
The Honourable Justice Chris Selochan
Judge of the Supreme Court
13
court: Supreme Court of Turks and Caicos Islands
summary: An 18‑year‑old convicted of penetrative sexual activity with a 13‑year‑old sentenced to four years' imprisonment per count, concurrent.

title: R v Michael Misick; R v Thomas Chalmers Misick; R v McAllister Hanchell (CR 18 of 2014; CR 35 of 2012; CR 36 of 2012) [2026] TCASC 11 (4 February 2026)
text:
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF
THE TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
Action No: CR 35/12
CR 36/12
CR 18/14
BETWEEN
THE KING
AND
MICHAEL MISICK
MC ALLISTER HANCHELL
THOMAS CHALMERS MISICK
BEFORE: RAJENDRA NARINE J
APPEARANCES:
MR. ANDREW MITCHELL KC AND MR. QUINN HAWKINS AND MS. N
GOMERSALL FOR THE CROWN
MR. GILBERT PETERSON SC, MR. ADRIAN KAYNE AND MR.
JAHMAL MISICK FOR MICHAEL MISICK
MR. JEROME LYNCH KC, MR. JAMES SHEPHERD AND MR.
LEMELKO MISSICK FOR MC ALLISTER HANCHELL
MR. OLIVER SMITH KC, MR. KAYODE SMITH, AND MR. JAHMAL
MISICK FOR THOMAS CHALMERS MISICK.
DECISION
1

1. The first defendant Michael Eugene Misick (MM) was the Chief Minister
and subsequently the first Premier of the Turks and Caicos Islands (TCI).
He is charged with three counts of bribery.
2. The second defendant, Mc Allister Eugene Hanchell (McH) was a former
minister of government of TCI. He is charged with two counts of bribery.
3. The third defendant, Thomas Chalmers Misick (TCM), is the brother of MM,
and was a practicing attorney-at-law in the TCI. He is charged with four
counts of concealing or disguising the proceeds of criminal conduct, the
criminal conduct being that of MM and McH.
AGREED FACTS – CONSTITUTIONAL AND GENERAL MATTERS
4. TCI is a British Overseas Territory. The King of England is the Head of
State. At the time of the alleged offences, Queen Elizabeth the Second was
the Head of State. The Head of Government was the Chief Minister who
became the Premier after the 2006 Constitution came into force on 9th
August 2006. The Queen reigned over the TCI through a Governor
appointed by the Queen on the advice of the Secretary of State of the
Foreign Commonwealth Office (FCO). During the period 11th July 2005 to
14th July 2008 Richard Tauwhare (RT) was the Governor of TCI. The
Governor reports to the Director of the Overseas Territories Directorate
(OTD) in the FCO.
5. By section 6(i) of the 1988 Constitution the executive authority of the TCI
was vested in the Queen who exercised that authority through the Governor.
Section 7 created the Executive Council (ExCo), which consisted of the
Governor, a Chief Minister appointed by the Governor, five ministers
appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Chief Minister, the Chief
2

Secretary, and the Attorney-General (AG). The Governor presides over the
ExCo. The members all had equal standing during ExCo meetings.
6. The Governor was required by Section 5 of the 1988 Constitution to consult
the ExCo on the formulation of policy and in the exercise of all functions
conferred upon him except when acting under instructions given to him by
the Secretary of State.
7. Under Section 13, the Governor was responsible for defence, external affairs,
international and offshore financial relations, resources and services, and
internal security, including the police force and the administration of the
public service.
8. The 1988 Constitution was replaced by the 2006 Constitution which came
into force on 9th August 2006. The ExCo was replaced by the Cabinet and
the Chief Minister was now to be known as the Premier.
9. The 2006 Constitution was partially suspended by the United Kingdom
Government in 2009, and was replaced by a new Constitution in November
2011.
10. The Constitution requires that Cabinet give its decision in the form of advice
to the Governor who normally accepts and acts on the advice except where
the Constitution provides to the contrary. The Cabinet minutes use the form
of language “H.E. the Governor concurred and ordered accordingly”.
11. In 1998 a document entitled “Responsibilities and Procedures for ExCo and
Government Business” was published. In part 5 paragraph 32 included a
term that if matters were not on the agenda, they could only be considered
with the Governor’s consent and only if the matter was exceptionally
3

urgent. It was also provided that the ExCo would not normally be expected
to decide on policy matters on a subject raised orally.
12. All members of the ExCo were jointly responsible for all decisions under
the principle of “collective responsibility”. Each member was expected to
participate fully in discussions of proposals for decision whether in favor or
against. However, once a decision was reached members were obliged to
accept responsibility for the decision even if they held strong views against
it. The alternative was to resign.
13. The 2006 Constitution changed the Chief Minister’s title to Premier, and
ExCo’s to Cabinet, which consisted of the Governor, the Premier, the A.G.
and six ministers. One of the six ministers was designated as Deputy
Premier. Members of the Cabinet were also members of the House of
Assembly which consisted of the Speaker, 15 elected members, 4 appointed
members and the A.G. Under the 1988 Constitution the legislature was
known as The Legislative Council.
14. Cabinet meetings normally took place on a Wednesday. The Clerk to the
Cabinet gave the Governor draft copies of the cabinet papers five or six days
before the meeting. Cabinet papers were discussed in advance of the
meeting by the Governor with the AG and the Deputy Governor (who was
the Chief Secretary before 2006), and separately, in a bilateral meeting by
the Governor with the Chief Minister/Premier the day before the cabinet
meeting. These facts were denied by McH in the schedule of agreed facts.
However, they are supported by the evidence of RT.
15. The Premier chaired a “pre-cabinet” meeting with his ministers to discuss
issues and co-ordinate their views amongst themselves. This fact was
denied by McH, but supported by the evidence of RT.
4

16. Issues raised orally at Cabinet meetings without a supporting Cabinet paper
were referred to as “Oral Mentions”. Sometimes other documentation such
as correspondence from attorneys acting on behalf of a developer was made
available for consideration at oral mentions. These facts were denied by
McH, but were given in evidence by RT.
17. After Cabinet meetings, the Clerk to the Cabinet prepared draft minutes for
the Governor to approve before they were presented at the next meeting.
Once approved the Clerk issued Action Minutes to the person at the relevant
ministry responsible for putting the decision into effect.
18. The policy of ExCo/Cabinet requires that members who had an interest in
any matter before it (including the allocation of Crown Land), that member
was required to declare his interest and withdraw from the meeting. (This
was denied by McH).
19. The approval of ministers in Cabinet was required for funds falling under
the Governor’s special areas of responsibility. The Governor needed to
secure the agreement of the ministers for such funding. The TCI
Government received no funding from the UK Government except for
specific projects such as improvements to the prison. (These facts were
denied by McH.)
20. There are two main political parties in the TCI. The Progressive National
Party (PNP) and the People’s Democratic Movement (PDM). The PDM
was in power from 1995 to August 2003, when the PNP won the general
elections, and remained in power until 2009.
21. Floyd Hall was the Treasurer of the PNP as at 21st November 1997 up to
April 2010.
5

22. The PNP held two bank accounts – First Caribbean International Bank
(FCIB) Account No. 1537005 and Belize Bank Turks & Caicos Limited
(BB) Account No. 5001837 which was opened in June 2002. The four
signatories to the FCIB account were Floyd Hall, Trevor Cooke, Arabella
Smith and MM as at 6th November 1997 until April 2010. The signatories
on the BB account were MM, Floyd Hall and Arabella Smith. The account
application stated that the account was opened for the purpose of depositing
the party’s money and paying the party’s bills, and that the funds will be
used for campaign purposes and operating the party’s offices.
23. In November 2006 a FCIB account no. 10391978 was opened in the name
of South Caicos 2007 PNP.
24. In 2003 the PNP campaigned on the issue of corruption. In its manifesto it
promised to pass anticorruption legislation, and to ensure that ministers and
holders of public office do not accept gifts or hospitality nor in any way
compromise, or appear to compromise their office or make them beholden
to any individual company or entity.
25. MM took his oath as a member of the legislative council on 9th May 2003,
and as Chief Minister on 15th August 2003.
26. McH took his oath of office on 20th August 2003. He was appointed
Minister of Communications, Works and Utilities on 25th August 2003.
27. In September 2004 by oral mention in the ExCo the annual salary of the
Chief Minister was determined to be $125,000.00, and that of Minister
$115,000.00.
28. On 6th February 2006, McH was appointed Minister of Natural Resources
with responsibility for the Department of Conservation, Environment and
6

Coastal Resources, (DECR), Land Registry, Lands and Survey, Land
Valuation and Agriculture.
29. On 9th February 2007 the PNP was re-elected, winning 13 out of 15 seats.
On 12th February 2007, MM was appointed Premier and swore his oath of
allegiance. On the same day McH was sworn in as Minister of Natural
Resources, Fishing and Environment. On 23rd February 2007, MM and
McH became members of the House of Assembly.
30. On 18th April 2007 the annual salary of the Premier was determined by
Cabinet to be $150,000.00 plus certain benefits, and that of ministers to be
$125,000.00 plus $5,000.00 per month in allowances.
31. On 6th February 2008, a new ministerial code was accepted by Cabinet, but
was not brought into force prior to the departure of RT.
32. In 2007 an Integrity Bill was drafted and eventually passed as the Integrity
Commission Ordinance 2008 on 24th April 2008.
33. On 17th February 2009, McH became responsible for the Planning
Department.
34. The Gazette was the official government publication of legal and other
notices. The Responsibilities and Procedures for Executive Council and
Government Business was gazetted on 28th August 1998. Each member of
the ExCo was provided with a copy following an election.
35. Each member of the legislature (including ministers and the Chief Minister)
was required to file an annual report of their interests in compliance with
the House of Assembly (Registration of Interest) Ordinance 1993, detailing
his interests, and those of his spouse and children, together with his income
7

to the Registrar of Interests who collated and published them. In default,
the member may be referred to a select committee which may require that
defaulting member to comply. If the member made a false statement in a
declaration, he was liable to be fined up to $5,000.00 or suspended from
sitting and voting in the House of Assembly. Registers were kept for the
years 2003-2009.
36. The AG had the prime function of legal adviser to the Cabinet and was
responsible for issues relating to the legal profession, technical legislation,
the judiciary and the courts. David Jeremiah was the AG from 1997 to 2004
followed by Kurt De Freitas from January 2005. Rhondalee Braithwaite-
Knowles (RBK) the present AG, became Crown Counsel in the AG’s
Chambers in 2000, Principal Crown Counsel in 2006, and Deputy AG in
2008.
37. Each minister was supported by a senior public official called a Permanent
Secretary (PS).
BELONGERSHIP
38. The term “Belongership” was introduced by the Immigration Ordinance 13
of 1992. Up until 2012 it could be acquired in the following ways:
(i) By a person who was born in the TCI and at least one parent held
Belonger status or was born in the TCI;
(ii) By a person who was born outside the TCI and lawfully adopted by
a Belonger;
(iii) By the grant of a Certificate of Belonger status by the Govenor for
having made an outstanding contribution to the social or economic
development of the TCI;
(iv) By a person who is the dependent child of a Belonger, and
8

(v) By a spouse of a Belonger, who has made an application to the
Governor, and has lived with the Belonger for five consecutive
years.
39. A Belonger had the exclusive right to vote at a general election, sit on a
jury, apply for a scholarship, acquire certain types of business licences, and
purchase Crown Land at a discount.
40. Applications for Belonger status were placed before Cabinet for decision.
Ministers applied their own subjective criteria as to what constituted an
outstanding contribution to the economic and social development of the
Islands which was not defined in the Immigration Ordinance. The role of
the Governor was limited to checking for previous criminal convictions and
any past involvement with the TCI.
41. Applications for Belongership were placed before Cabinet by the
Permanent Residence Unit (PRU). Once agreed in principle by Cabinet, the
application was returned to the PRU, which published notice of the intended
award in the Gazette to provide an opportunity to members of the public to
object to the application. If no objections were received after two
consecutive advertisements, a Belonger Certificate was prepared by the
PRU for the Governor’s signature.
42. Mario Hoffman was granted Belonger status on 17th November 2006.
43. LisaRaye McCoy, MM’s wife, was proposed for Belonger status and
accepted by Cabinet on 7th March 2007. The decision was re-affirmed on
27th February 2008.
CROWN LAND
9

44. The Registered Land Ordinance requires that all land be registered either as
Crown Land or private land. Crown Land is defined by Section 2 of the
Physical Planning Ordinance 1998 as land of which the Crown is owner, or
is registered as the lessee under the Ordinance, or which is otherwise vested
in the Crown. Section 94 of the 2006 Constitution vested Crown Land in
the Governor who may dispose of it on behalf of the Crown.
45. Crown Land Policies (CLP) were issued in 1994 and 2005. The 1994 CLP
was updated and amended with effect from 1st April 2004. The 2005 CLP
was effective from 2nd November 2005, and was amended in early 2008.
46. Paragraphs 4.1 and 4.2 of the 1994 CLP expressed the commitment of the
Government to develop the TCI in order to improve the standard of living
of the Belonger population. As part of the development strategy, the
Government intended to use Crown Land for commercial and residential
development to encourage Belonger participation in the economy to benefit
the Belonger population and the community as a whole.
47. Developments of Crown Land were divided into the following categories:
(i) Residential;
(ii) Belonger only small commercial developments;
(iii) Belonger only large commercial developments;
(iv) Non-belonger developments, and
(v) Joint ventures.
48. Shaaban Hoza was the Chief Valuation Officer for TCI.
49. The Surveys and Mapping Department was formerly known as the Lands
and Surveys Department. It was within the Ministry of Environment and
District Administration, formerly the Ministry of Natural Resources. This
department was responsible for mapping the TCI, maintaining up to date
10

records of all land information and ensuring that surveys of Crown Land
were correctly executed.
50. On each of the Islands the land is divided into a number of contiguous
blocks. Each block is numbered. Each parcel has its own unique reference
number.
51. Applications for Crown Land were filed either at the Lands and Survey
Department or at various other offices at the Ministry. They were recorded
on a data base and forwarded to the PS of the Ministry of Natural Resources.
The PS or the Minister would then indicate which applications should be
processed.
52. From 2002 Conrad Higgs (now deceased) was the Chairman of TCInvest, a
statutory body established by the Turks and Caicos Islands Investment
Agency Ordinance 1975. TCInvest was concerned with promoting and
facilitating inward investment. According to Conrad Higgs and RBK,
TCInvest and the AG Chambers were supposed to be involved in the “front
end” of the registration of a Development Agreement (DA) with the
Attorneys for the developer. However, it not infrequently happened that
developers approached the Ministers before their proposals were referred to
TCInvest.
53. Clyde Robinson was the Director of Planning from 2003 to 2010. He
reported to the PS of the relevant ministry. Before 2003, that was the office
of Home Affairs. From 2003, it was the Premier’s Office until it came under
the auspices of the Ministry of Environment where McH was the
responsible minister.
54. The role of the Planning Department was to check planning applications for
compliance with the Planning Ordinance, standards and building
11

regulations. The Planning Department consulted with the DECR and other
relevant agencies. Once all the information has been submitted and advice
taken from the Planning Officer, the Director makes a recommendation to
the physical Planning Board either:
(a) To approve the application, or
(b) To approve with conditions, or
(c) To reject the application.
55. The Physical Planning Board (PPB) was a statutory body of not less than 7
members appointed by the Governor. The PPB usually met once a month.
Appeals from decisions of the PPB were dealt with by the Minister of
Planning.
56. When Crown Land was allocated to a developer, Cabinet was required to
approve, in outline, the terms of a Development Agreement (DA), including
concessions such as reductions in stamp duty, import duties and guarantees
of work permits for the construction staff. If the outline was approved, the
AG Chambers, the department responsible for protecting the interests of the
government, would take that as their basis for negotiations with the
developer.
57. Generally, DAs required the developers to invest a particular sum in the
project, setting out the phases of the development to be completed within a
set time-frame. When the final draft of a DA was completed, the AG
presented it to the Governor for signature. Proposed changes to the DA
were referred to Cabinet for approval, before being signed by the Governor.
Some proposed developments raised environmental issues or encroached
upon protected areas. All major commercial developments required an
Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) to be conducted to identify any
environmental issues, before the terms of the DA were concluded. (These
particular facts were denied by McH.)
12

58. On 1st April 2004 the 1994 CLP was updated with several amendments in
keeping with the government’s policy to empower Belongers. The amount
of land that would be allocated to a Belonger was increased from one acre
to one acre per island, provided that a home was completed on another
island.
59. The Minister of Natural Resources was given power to allocate land without
the matter being considered by the full ExCo, provided that the sub-division
of the land had previously been approved by ExCo.
60. On 2nd November 2005 a new CLP took effect. The new CLP set out a list
of 21 key principles to be considered in allocation decisions. It was
formulated to provide long-term benefits to Belongers by increasing their
role in commercial land development and broadening the availability of
land for residential use. The stated intention was expressed as
“Empowering our people, building our nation”.
61. The 2005 CLP provided for a maximum discount of 50% for Belongers on
the purchase price of Crown Land for residential purposes. If the land was
sold within 5 years, the full discount was to be repaid. If sold within 5-10
years, half the discount became repayable.
62. With respect to commercial developments, the 2005 CLP provided that no
individual Belonger would be granted a discounted on more than 10 acres.
For large developments, two or more Belongers were permitted to combine
their 10 acre entitlements. The discount of 50% did not apply to
Providenciales, where a discount of 25% was applicable. In the case of joint
ventures, the policy provided that Belongers must have at least a 51%
participation for the joint venture to qualify for a discount. Only the portion
of land to be purchased by Belongers would qualify for the discount. The
13

policy further required that new large-scale developments would be subject
to review and comment by the Planning Department and the DECR, and
their input would be included in the DA to be signed by the Governor and
the developer. The new requirements for open tendering and 51% Belonger
interest did not apply to projects already under consideration.
63. There was a practice underpinned and permitted by primary legislation, of
granting concessions to major commercial developers in order to attract
investment.
64. Building in protected areas was constrained by the CLP. These included
areas of significant ecological or historical importance. Paragraph 13 of the
2005 CLP provided that these areas will not be allocated for development.
65. On 23rd August 2006, Cabinet delegated authority to the Minister of Natural
Resources to renew offers on commercial CLPs provided that the Planning,
Lands and Survey and valuation conditions remain unchanged and the land
did not exceed 10 acres. The Minister was also authorized to approve
freehold titles on residential and commercial leases of Crown Land once the
lessee was in possession of a letter of commitment from a lending
institution. On 6th September 2006 Cabinet further delegated authority to
the minister to authorize freehold title on offer letters before CLPs were
executed, once the lessee was in possession of a letter of commitment from
a lending institution.
66. In April 2008, it was agreed that an independent review of the allocation of
Crown Land on Salt Cay, be conducted by Deloitte and Touche. The report
was completed in June 2008, and discussed in Cabinet on 3rd July 2008. It
did not find any evidence of wrongdoing by the developers.
14

67. On 11th June 2008, approval was granted to allow persons holding offer
letters of residential and commercial leases dated on or before that date, to
be granted an option to drawdown on the freehold title to that land (without
the requirements of a letter from a financial institution or a building up to
belt beam) at an additional 10% on the existing offer. The offer was valid
until 31st December 2008.
68. On 10th July 2008, the Governor of TCI announced a Commission of
Inquiry (COI). The COI’s interim report was submitted on 23rd February
2009. A final report was submitted on 31st May 2009.
69. MM resigned on 23rd March 2009.
AGREED LEGAL DIRECTIONS
70. In accordance with the current practice in several jurisdictions including the
UK, the attorneys have agreed legal directions. The general directions are
set out below. The directions that apply to the specific offences charged
will be set out and applied later in the judgment when the Court deals with
the specific counts on the indictment.
71. The burden is on the Crown to prove that each defendant is guilty of the
offences charged. The standard of proof required is that the Crown must
prove their case beyond reasonable doubt, that is to make the court sure of
the guilt of the accused. There is no burden on any of the defendants to
prove his innocence. Indeed, there is no burden on any defendant to prove
any issue in the case.
72. The court must consider the case of each defendant separately on each count
on the indictment, and must return separate verdicts on each count against
each defendant. Where a defendant faces more than one count the court
15

must consider each count separately and return separate verdicts on each
count. In addition, where a defendant is charged together with another
defendant on the same count, the court must consider the case against each
defendant separately and deliver separate verdicts for each defendant.
73. The defendants elected to remain silent, that is, not to give evidence. This
is their right. They are entitled to remain silent and argue that the
prosecution has not proved the case against them. No adverse inference
may be drawn against the defendant from exercising his right to remain
silent. In this case it is not in dispute that the defendants have no previous
convictions and are of good character.
74. The principles set out by the English Court of Appeal in R v Vye [1993] 1
WLR 471 establish that the good character of a defendant must be taken
into consideration in two ways:
(i) In assessing his credibility that is whether or not he is speaking the
truth, and;
(ii) In assessing his propensity, that is the likelihood that a man of good
character will commit the offence for which he is charged.
75. Good character supports the credibility of a defendant. It must be taken into
account where a defendant has given evidence or has given pre-trial
statements to the police, whether in interview or otherwise. In this case,
none of the defendants have given pre-trial statements and have all elected
not to give evidence. It follows that there is no evidence or pre-trial
statements to which the credibility limb of the Vye direction can be applied.
76. However, all counsel argue, and the court concurs that the propensity limb
must be considered in relation to all three defendants. The court must
consider that it is less likely that a defendant who has no previous
convictions, and against whom no allegations of previous misconduct or
16

offending against the criminal law have been made, is less likely to commit
the offence for which he is charged. This principle will be applied to each
defendant on each count.
77. The events on which this case is based took place in some cases, more than
20 years ago. The court must consider the effect of the passage of time on
the memories of the witnesses, particularly where the evidence is not
supported by contemporaneous documents. In addition, while there is no
direct evidence on the availability of witnesses, the court must consider that
the defendants may not have been able to call witnesses who may have died,
or cannot be traced, or who are now unable to recall events or details of
certain matters. In addition, documents that may have been useful may now
be lost or cannot be traced.
78. The court must further consider whether or not the defendants might have
been disadvantaged by their inability to cross-examine witnesses, who due
to age or infirmity or death, did not appear at the trial and whose testimony
at the aborted trial before Harrison J. was played at this trial.
79. Where the delay has placed a defendant at a material disadvantage in
meeting the case against him, the court must take this into account in his
favor: R v Hewitt [2020] EWCA 1247.
80. Certain facts were agreed or undisputed by the parties. The facts set out in
the updated schedule of facts are accepted by the parties as the truth of the
fact stated, without necessarily accepting that it was within their knowledge
or suspicion at the time and “subject to errors or omissions, and/or subject
to further explanations and/or clarification elicited from any witness”
during the course of the trial, or relevance to the issues being tried.
17

81. The statements of Conrad Higgs were read into the record because he is
deceased. The admission of the statements did not mean that the Crown
and the defence agree that all the contents are true. It is for the court to
decide on the weight to be attached to the statements bearing in mind the
following limitations:
(i) The statements were not made under oath or affirmation, albeit that
Conrad Higgs signed a formal declaration at the beginning of each
of his statements that his statement was true and he knew that he
could be prosecuted if he deliberately made a statement that was
false.
(ii) The defence were deprived of the opportunity to cross-examine the
witness, which may have shaken the credibility or reliability of the
evidence.
(iii) In deciding the weight to be attached to the evidence the court must
consider it in the light of other evidence in the case.
82. The Crown’s case relies heavily on circumstantial evidence. The directions
